4.12.28.         Figure R.09 - The Basics of Roles – An Example

Roles represent a fundamental way to provide extensibility in the model. The Role Object pattern [ROLEOBJ] is used to extend the use and
application of an object. For example, by modeling the different functions that a device can play as a set of objects.

A role characterizes the function that a particular object plays. In the SID, the characterization may be to specify attributes and relationships.

Roles provide an extensible means of abstraction that enables the user to model the functions played by an entity as separate objects (instead of
embedding that functionality in the entity itself). For example, suppose that you are modeling the business functions that your employees have. Basic
organizational boundaries define some behavior (e.g., marketing vs. sales vs. engineering), but often employees play multiple roles (e.g., an engineer
that has sales support functions). This phenomenon doesn’t just occur for people – other managed objects exhibit it as well. For example, in a VPN,
some DeviceInterfaces talk to a customer, while others never touch a customer.

In general, managed objects don’t all have the same behavior, but different managed objects may have some common behavior. Worse, an object
might exhibit more than one set of common behaviors, or may take on and remove different behaviors during its lifetime. Thus, we need a means to
capture these and related cases. A set of role patterns provide an extensible and straightforward means of accomplishing this goal.

Before these different role patterns are discussed, it is important to differentiate between an instance or a type. Asking an object if it is an instance of
type is something different than asking it if it is an instance of a class, since the types (interfaces) and the classes (implementations) can be different.
(Note that there are patterns to accomplish this function.)

The SID is concerned with types, not implementation.

There are five types of role patterns that are extensions of [ROLEOBJ], described as follows:

•     Single Role Type. This pattern combines all of the features of different roles into a single type, and is most useful for modeling objects having a
      few variations on the same basic behavior.

•     Separate Role Type. This pattern models each role as its own separate type, and is most useful for modeling objects that have little if any
      common behavior.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                  333 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     Role Subtype. This pattern models each role that exhibits different behavior as a subtype. Note that this does NOT mean that sub-classing is
      used, as this is an analysis pattern and is thus concerned with interfaces, not implementation. This pattern is all about maintaining the illusion that
      clients are dealing with a single object that has multiple, changeable types.

•     Role Object. This pattern models each role that exhibits different behavior as a separate object. The separate role objects are then related to a
      single base object that ties the different behaviors together. This pattern is used when maintaining the illusions of a single object is too difficult, or
      doesn’t supply the correct semantics.

•     Role Relationship. This pattern models each role as a relationship with an appropriate object.

Of these five patterns, the SID uses the fourth – Role Object – almost exclusively. Single Role Type and Role Type aren’t used because they best
deal with behavior which is not included in the SID.

This leaves Role Subtype and Role Relationship. The problem with the Role Subtype pattern (in the context of the SID) is that this pattern cannot be
used if there are multiple and/or changing roles.

The Role Relationship pattern is not used in the SID due to its complexity. This pattern is very well suited when the role itself embodies a relationship
between a base object and some other object (usually a group). For example, consider an organization which requires new employees to spend an
equal amount of time working for multiple departments. In this case, the role binds the person to the particular organization that he or she is currently
working for. Thus, it is best modeled as a relationship between the person and the organization that the person is working for.

Figure below shows a simplified view of the concepts of using the Role Object pattern.

The figure shows that an Individual (or an Organization) can be assigned zero or more roles. Thus, an Employee can take on the roles of
ServiceProvider and Partner.

The idea is to define the functionality that a business entity can play as a set of subclasses of EntityRole, and then associate the specific functionality
that is desired using the EntityHasRoles aggregation. This enables the functionality and behavior of the business entity to be easily customized. For
example, consider two different people working in the same department – both interact with a customer, but Bob is a manager and Joe isn’t. Rather
than add employee-specific attributes to the definition of a Person, a Person can be kept general, and employee-specific attributes can be added to
an appropriate role.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                      334 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



More formally, roles recognize the fact that different entities may have different roles that it can play when it collaborates with other entities in the
system. Separating this into different roles enables behavior to be defined that involves just the role, as opposed to all of the attributes of the entity.
This enables the communication to become more specific and thus easier to generalize.

Different Types of Roles in SID

The SID is unique, in that roles are used in the vast majority of its domains, to ensure that the above philosophy is applied wherever it makes sense.
Three different examples that show the diversity of roles in the SID are as follows:

•     Party and PartyRole (Party addendum). Party is a superclass for modeling people and organizations, and PartyRole is a superclass for modeling
      the different jobs that people can have.

•     Resource and ResourceRole (Physical Resource addendum and Logical and Compound Resource addendum). Resources are physical, logical,
      and compound resource entities that represent the definition, development, and operational aspects of the information computing and processing
      infrastructure of a system. There are PhysicalResourceRoles as well as LogicalResourceRoles, which enable functions associated with the
      physical and logical aspects, respectively, of a resource to be modelled independent of that resource. There are also CompoundResourceRoles,
      which enable roles to be assigned to CompoundResources (Resources that contain one or more PhysicalResources and LogicalResources).

•     Service and ServiceRole (Service Overview addendum and Quality of Service addendum). Services represent intangible functionality that is
      provided by the information computing and processing infrastructure of a system. CustomerFacingServiceRoles represent abstracted functionality
      that represent how a Product is realized, while ResourceFacingServiceRoles represent abstracted functionality that are required in order for the
      CustomerFacingService to function correctly.

All of these are currently defined in various GB922 Addenda as noted above.

4.12.30.        Figure R.11 - ManagementDomain

ManagementDomain represents a special grouping of ManagedEntities that has two important properties. First, it is used to partition managed objects
into a meaningful logical grouping. One important use of such a grouping is to provide a means to define which EMS (as well as which NMS)
manages, monitors, etc. which set of devices. Another important use of such a grouping is to provide a means to show how management functions
are distributed and scaled.

Second, this grouping defines a common administrative domain that is used to administer the managed objects that it contains. This implies that all of
the managed objects contained in this ManagementDomain are administered similarly – either by the same user, group of users or policy.

A ManagementDomain is the superclass of several important entities in the SID. One of the most important of these is the PolicyDomain class. A
PolicyDomain is a collection of entities and services that are administered in a coordinated fashion using a set of policies. The policies are used to
control the set of services and entities according to a common methodology, such as a finite state machine. This is defined in Policy addendum.

The ManagementDomain class also enables relationships to be defined that convey various semantics, such as containment, scoping, administration,
and more generic management functions.

4.12.31.        Figure R.12 - ManagementInfo Class Hierarchy

ManagementInfo is an abstract class that is used to represent entities that represent management information obtained in a managed environment.
Specifically, in the process of managing an entity, information of various forms is created. This class is the superclass for defining different types of
management information. Its main subclasses are shown in the Figure below.

The design of how management information is obtained uses this class to gather specific types of information, and the ManagementMethodEntity
class to obtain the desired data using an appropriate method. This combination of class hierarchies enables different management methods (e.g., CLI
vs. SNMP) to be used to gather different types of management data, and is unique to the SID information model.

                                                                                    +   retrievalMethodCurrent: Integer

4.12.32.        Figure R.13 - ManagementMethodEntity Class Hierarchy

ManagementMethodEntity is an abstract class for representing the different types of management methods that can be used to gather a particular
type of management information. For example, this enables configuration setting classes to be defined as using CLI, while configuration monitoring
classes could be defined as using a different method, such as SNMP. Its main subclasses are shown in the Figure below.

The design of how management information is obtained uses this class to gather specific types of information, and the ManagementMethodEntity
class to obtain the desired data using an appropriate method. This combination of class hierarchies enables different management methods (e.g., CLI
vs. SNMP) to be used to gather different types of management data, and is unique to the SID information model.

Figure R.04 – ManagementInformation and Methods for Obtaining Management Data showed how ManagementInfo and ManagementMethodEntity
work together to provide a single, scalable solution for gathering different types of management information that describe a management entity. This
is especially important for some types of network devices, where different protocols and approaches are used to configure vs. monitor the device.

4.12.33.         Figure R.14 - PartyIdentification Excerpt (taken from Party Diagrams)

Identification

Business entities consist of two types – those that are simple values, such as a quantity of money or a period of time, and those that have identity.
For these latter entities, the concept of an identity is used to distinguish different instances of the same object.

Identity is flexibly and extensibly defined using the group of objects defined in this chapter.

Individual people, places, and things, such as customer orders, equipment, network elements and so forth, can be recognized (identified) by different
attributes. The business will define (usually through a policy) what particular set of attributes or concepts is appropriate for a given entity. For
example, an individual person may be identified using a passport number, a driver’s license number, or a national identification number. Similarly, a
network location may be identified by a network node ID or a Location Entity Identification Code (as defined in American National Standard T1.253).

It should be noted that policy does not have to be used. However, the use of policy provides an elegant and extensible means to define the semantics
of using an identity. This is done by modeling associations between PolicyRules and the identification entities that it relates to.

Figure EI.01 – PartyIdentification Excerpt (taken from Party addendum) below shows an excerpt for identifying different types of Parties (e.g.,
Individuals and Organizations).

The manner in which each key business entity in the SID model is identified could be modeled in a way similar to Party. However, each business
entity can be identified in a number of ways, as shown in the example above for individuals. Modeling the identity in this way means that each key
entity would have a set of classes similar to those used to model the identities of Party. This introduces redundancy into the SID model for every
entity that requires identification. In addition, using explicit subclasses to model the different types of identification attributes for an entity results in a
model that cannot be easily extended. This is because as new ways to identify an entity are defined or discovered, a new subclass must be
introduced into the SID model. Having to introduce new subclasses implies that the parts of the SID model that represent entity identifications are not
very stable. It also makes it difficult for other teams and I to adopt the SID, since its baseline is always changing.

4.12.34.        Figure R.15 - Beginning of the Entity Identification ABE Model

To eliminate the redundancy and instability within the model caused by the repeated modeling of similar identification structures and by introducing
subclasses for each way that an entity can be identified, the EntitySpecification – Entity pattern is used. Here, the “EntitySpecification” part of the
pattern will take the form of a specification for identifying Entities, and the “Entity” part of the pattern will take the form of an object that can be used to
identify entities.

Therefore, applying this pattern yields the beginning of a model for Entity identification, as shown in Figure EI.02 – Beginning of the Entity
Identification ABE Model below.

An EntityIdentificationSpec defines the attributes that are shared by every instance of a related EntityIdentification. (and methods, constraints,
relationships and behavior in the system view) of an EntityIdentification object. Note that methods and constraints (as specified in OCL) are useful for
specifying “static” behavior (e.g., functionality that can be defined at design time). Thus, “behavior” refers to dynamic functionality and runtime
considerations that are not easy to represent in a class model. For example, the directive to instantiate an object only in response to a run-time
exception is easy to conceptualize as a constraint, but is difficult to model in UML. Other examples include the specification of pre- and post-
conditions and other contract related data.

                                                                                                   +       description: String
                                                                                                   +       name: String
                                                                                                   +       validFor: TimePeriod

                                                                                                                                0..*

                                                                    EntityRecognizedUsing                        EntityIdentification
                                                   Entity

                                                                                                       +    value: String

specifying “static” behavior (e.g., functionality that can be defined at design time). Thus, “behavior” refers to dynamic functionality and runtime
considerations that are not easy to represent in a class model. For example, the directive to instantiate an object only in response to a run-time
exception is easy to conceptualize as a constraint, but is difficult to model in UML. Other examples include the specification of pre- and post-

4.12.36.        Figure EI.04 – EntityIdentificationSpecification Examples

An EntityIdentificationSpecification represents one or more attributes (not attribute values) used to recognize (identify) an instance of an Entity.
Figure EI.04 – EntityIdentificationSpecification Examples below shows an 8-byte Network Site Format using ANS T1.253.

Note that the various component specifications (Geographical Code, Geopolitical Code, and Network Site Code) that make up a complete Network
Node ID, which is represented as an CompositeEntityIdentificationSpec object, are represented as instances of the
ComponentEntityIdentificationSpec, and are aggregated by the CompositeEntityIdentificationSpec.

4.12.37.        Figure R.17 - The EntityIdentificationSpecificationFormat Association Class

The component specification must be able to occur in a certain sequence to define the composite. And, they may be preceded and/or followed by
certain characters. For example, the format of a phone number could contain dashes, such as 972-477-8401. Figure EI.05 – The
EntityIdentificationSpecificationFormat Association Class below shows an association class added to hold the sequence in which the components
appear in a composite object, along with its formatting attributes.

4.12.38.        Figure R.18 - Identifying Entities Using an Associated EntitySpecification

The next thing that must be known about an EntityIdentitySpecification is what type of business Entity the EntityIdentitySpecification applies to. There
are two different means of doing this. The difference is whether the Entity being identified has an associated EntitySpecification or not. For example, a
Market Entity has no associated Specification, whereas a Resource Entity or a Service Entity often do have an associated EntitySpecification.

The first option is shown in Figure EI.06 – Identifying Entities Using an Associated EntitySpecification. In this option, an EntitySpecification defines a
Entity as well as a set of EntityIdentificationSpecs. Any of these EntityIdentitySpecs may be used to identify the EntityIdentification object that is used
to identify the associated Entity. Looking at Figure EI.06 – Identifying Entities Using an Associated EntitySpecification below, we see that the
EntitySpecification that characterizes the Entity contains, in addition to its invariant attributes, zero or more EntityIdentificationSpecs. The
EntityIdentitySpecification in turn specifies the set of EntityIdentification objects that can be used to identify the Entity that the EntitySpecification
applies to. This enables the EntitySpecification to in effect direct the usage of a subordinate specification (the EntityIdentitySpecification) to indirectly
define EntityIdentification objects that can be used to identify the Entity to which the EntitySpecification applies to, providing a great deal of inherent
flexibility in determining how the Entity will be identified.

                                                      EntitySpecification                             SpecifiesEntity                                    Entity
                                                  +   status: String               0..1                                                 0..*
                                                  +   validFor: TimePeriod                                                                                     1

                                                                    0..*
                                                                    EntitiesRecognizedBy                                                          EntityRecognizedUsing



                                                                    0..*                                                                                       0..*

                                              EntityIdentificationSpecification                                                                  EntityIdentification
                                                                                               EntityIdentitySpecificationDefines
                                         +   description: String                                                                    0..* +     value: String
                                                                                           1
                                         +   name: String
                                         +   validFor: TimePeriod

4.12.39.        Figure R.19 - Identifying Entities Using the RootEntityType Entity

The second option is shown in Figure EI.07 – Identifying Entities Using the RootEntityType Entity below. This option is used for Entities that don’t
have an associated EntitySpecification, and can also be used in the first case if desired. This option introduces an entity (called RootEntityType) into
the model whose instance is the business entity that is to be identified. Examples of business entities that use this approach include Location
(identified by the 8-byte Network Site Format), Individual, Organization, Customer, Supplier, Market Segment and so forth.

Entity above, is used to define the formatting and sequencing of EntityIdentificationSpec objects that are aggregated by a particular
EntityIdentificationSpecComposite object. The RootEntityTypeIdentificationSpec association class, shown in Figure EI.08 – Use of an Entity

4.12.42.        Figure R.22 - Entity and Entity Identification Example

Every instance of an entity has a unique identifier. Simple identifiers can be defined as attributes of an entity. In cases where one or more complex
identifiers are required, an object is required. This is implemented using an entity identification specification and its associated entity identification
objects. For example, ANS T1.253 specifies a complex identifier for a network location Figure EI.10 – Entity and Entity Identification below shows an
example of the EntityIdentificationSpecification, the EntityIdentification, and the RootEntity instance identified using this specification. (Note that the
multiplicity of the RootEntityRecognizedUsing association is 0..1 on the RootEntity side, and not 1, because our first option doesn’t use this approach;
therefore, RootEntity and RootEntityType must be kept optional.)

4.12.43.        1. Customer Site Format

This section contains additional examples that depict the use of the Entity Identification Specification and Entity Identification. The examples are
taken from the American National Standard T1.253-1999.



This is a standardized code that uniquely identifies a customer’s presence at a location at which network elements or network systems equipment
exist

Geographical Code

The designation for a single geographical locality within a state, province, territory, country, or distinct region of the world (e.g., municipality). This
data element shall consist of four alphabetic characters. New codes assigned shall contain exactly four characters and shall not contain spaces. For
previously assigned codes of less than four characters, the unused character positions after the code shall be filled with spaces to make up four
character positions.

Geopolitical Code

The designation of a state or territory of the United States, a province or territory of Canada, another country having a national federal government, or
a unique designation (e.g., the oceans). This data element shall consist of two alphabetic characters.

Customer Site Code

The designation of a customer that utilizes telecommunications equipment at a specific location where there is a requirement to uniquely identify that
customer’s presence for the purpose of inventory and facility management.

This data element shall consist of five characters having one numeric character in the first position followed by one alphabetic and three alphabetic or
numeric characters in that sequence.

Customer Site Format Table

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                 353 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



This format shall consist of a sequence of Geographical Code and Geopolitical Code data elements plus a Customer Site Code data element,
resulting in a code that totals eleven characters in the format shown in the table below.




                                                             1. Customer Site Format




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                       354 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.12.44.         2. Equipment Entity Identification Format

A standardized code that uniquely identifies a type of manufactured telecommunications network equipment or an assignable entity within a type of
equipment. The Equipment Entity Identification Format shall consist of a sequence of Family Code, Sub-Family Code, Features Code, Reference
Code and Complemental Code data elements, resulting in a code that totals ten characters in the following format.

Components

Family Code

A label to denote a functional category of manufactured telecommunications equipment. This data element shall consist of two characters, either of
which may be alphabetic or numeric. An example is D4, representing a D4 channel bank.

Sub-Family Code

A label to denote a functional category of manufactured telecommunications equipment within a family. This data element shall consist of two
characters, either of which may be alphabetic or numeric. An example is CE, representing an E&M signaling channel unit.

Features Code

A standardized code to denote a distinctive service-providing design capability available within a sub-family of manufactured telecommunication
equipment when compared to other similar equipment of the same or other manufacturers. This data element shall consist of three characters, each
of which may be alphabetic or numeric. Manufacturers’ examples are:

•     18B, where

•     1 signifies E&M signaling, 4 wire

•     8 signifies E with M lead applique, M lead resistor 510 ohm

•     B signifies with busy switch and level adjust attenuator

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                           355 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     424, where

•     4 signifies DX signaling, 4 wire with post equalization

•     2 signifies adjustable balance capacitance and resistance

•     4 signifies impedance 150/600/1200 ohm

•     363, where

•     3 signifies DX signaling, 2 wire with gain transfer

•     6 signifies eliminate signaling condition, fixed balance capacitance

•     3 signifies receive enable circuit

Reference Code

This is a label to denote the manufacturer’s information used to describe the design capability encoded in the equipment entity identification code.
This data element shall consist of one character, which may be alphabetic or numeric. Examples are:

•     7, signifying NNTM

•     8, signifying LEAR

•     P, signifying SD-7C026-02

Complemental Code

This is a standardized code to provide uniqueness to an equipment entity identification code. This data element shall consist of two characters, either
of which may be alphabetic or numeric. (Examples of typical information are manufacturing vintage, version data, circuit wiring, or equipment data.)
Examples are:

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              356 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     AA, for Northern Telecom, signifying NNTM QPP357B

•     AA, for LEAR, signifying LEAR D4DX-4010-11

•     AB, for Lucent Technologies, signifying J98726SD-2Composites

Basic Code

A Basic Code is comprised of the Family Code and Sub-Family Code data elements taken together, represented in the following format.

4.12.47.        Root Business Entities/Characteristic ABE Illustration

Figure Ch.01 – Characteristic Specification depicts the basic CharacteristicSpecification pattern. The pattern enables attributes unique to a type of
entity to be created via its association to EntitySpecification and enables attributes unique to a certain entity to be created via its association to
RootEntityType. A RootEntityType is an entity in the SID. For example, characteristics could be defined for entities (instances of RootEntityType)
such as Product, Customer, and ProductSpecification that apply to all instances of those entities. Instance diagrams are shown in Figure Ch.03 –
Characteristic Spec Instance – Example 1 and Figure Ch.04 – Characteristic Spec Instance – Example 2. Definitions of entities and attributes are
provided in the data dictionary at the end of this document.

An example of the use of the pattern will also be illustrated using the following figure.

The EntitySpecification, which could be a ProductSpecification, instance is for a Linksys USB Ethernet Cable Modem DOCSIS. Each tab, Features,
Description, Additional Info, and Shipping Info is an instance of CharacteristicSpecification. The Features CharacteristicSpec is a composite that is
comprised of an instance of CharacteristicSpec for each feature line. Instances of the CharacteristicSpecRelationship are used to compose the
Features of the instances of feature line.

In this example, the CharacteristicSpecValue and CharSpecValueRelationship entities are not used. An example of their use could be for a set of
coordinated colors for the modem. For example, if a choice for a faceplate color (CharacteristicSpec) is blue (CharacteristicSpecValue), then the only
color for the case color is black. This rule would be implemented by an instance of CharSpecValueRelationship that relates the color blue with the
color black with a value for charValueRelationshipType of “inclusive”.

See an example in Annex: More Details on Characteristics.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                               360 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Root Business Entities/Characteristic ABE Illustration




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                  361 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.12.48.         Figure R.23 - CharacteristicSpecification and Values

The minCardinality and maxCardinality attributes are used to specify the number of values that can be associated with an instance of a RootEntity. In
the example the faceplate color would have a minCardinality of 1 and a maxCardinality of 1. A minCardinality of 0 implies that a value does not have
to be assigned to a characteristic.

A valueType attribute exists for both CharacteristicSpecifications and CharacteristicSpecValue in order to have a mixture of value types for values.
For example, a CharacteristicSpecification may be enumerated by a combination of strings and integers. The valueType of the
CharacteristicSpecification is used if the valueType is the same for all CharacteristicSpecValue instances.

An instance CharacteristicSpecValue can represent discrete value or a range of values:

•     An example of a discrete value is the values that the CharacteristicSpecification “color” can take on.

•     An example of a range of values is the range of operating temperatures under which the cable modem can operate. A rangeInterval attribute for
      CharacteristicSpecValue defines whether the valueFrom and valueTo attributes are inclusive or not. This is equivalent to adding an “operator”
      attribute for valueFrom and an “operator” attribute for valueTo, such as “<=” or “<”. The rangeStep attribute specifies that a range of values
      increases in steps, such as a range from 0 to 100 in steps of 5 with the resulting permitted values being 0, 5, 10, 15,…100. This complements the
      valueFrom, valueTo, and rangeInterval attributes. Previously there was no way to do so, and individual CharacteristicSpecValues had to be
      created.

In some models the value attribute and the range attributes are contained within separate subclasses of the CharacteristicSpecValue entity. This was
not done in the Characteristic model in order to simplify it. Also, some models may contain unit of measure as a separate entity with a recursive
association and an association class that is used to define conversion factors between various units of measure. This was not done in the
Characteristic model. However, both the subclasses and unit of measure entity and association class could be modelled as extensions to the SID if
desired.

Some CharacteristicSpecifications are enumerated by a fixed value or set of values that remain constant for every instance of an Entity. For
example, the operating temperature range for the cable modem remains constant. The attribute canBeOverridden is used to indicate this.
Additionally, the set of CharacteristicSpecValues may be open to extension as values are assigned to instances of EntityCharacteristics. For
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              362 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



example, there may be a desire to keep a list of userIDs as instances of CharacteristicSpecValues in addition to their existence as instances of
CharacteristicValues. The extensible attribute is used to indicate that new instances of CharacteristicSpecValues can be added as instances of
CharacteristicValues are created.

More precisely:

Values associated with CharSpecs can never be changed when an entity's CharValue instances are created. They are fixed but can be extended if
the "extensible" attribute is true.

example 1:

In the case the xxxSpeccharUse is a temperature range <-20, +50>

•     If extensible = FALSE => the range cannot be changed for any instance

•     If extensible = TRUE => the range can be changed for a given instance into (as example) <-10, +40>

example 2:

In the case the xxxSpeccharUse is a list of enum: e.g. Color with values Black and Blue

•     canBeOverriden = FALSE => nothing can be changed, and instances will have both values Black and Blue

•     canBeOverriden = TRUE => one can chose Black OR Blue either for a given instance

example 3:

In the case the xxxSpecCharUse is a bandwidth range from 10 to 100 with a step of 10

•     If canBeOverridden = FALSE => the range cannot be changed for any instance




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                             363 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     If canBeOverridden = TRUE => the range still cannot be changed for any instance (e.g. we cannot select a subrange, like from 40 to 70 with
      same step of 10) but we can pick up one value (like 20) or we can select a set of values (like 30 and 60) from the range. The number of choices
      depends on the Min and Max attributes (in this case, the Min is 1 and the Max is 2.

CharacteristicSpecification does not use the composite/atomic pattern. Instead there is the recursive CharacteristicSpecRelationship association that
can be used to associate several CharacteristicSpecification.

The CharacteristicSpecRelationship association can be used to represent/emulate a composite CharacteristicSpecification associated with several
other CharacteristicSpecification. If used that way, the model does not restrict the possibility for the composite CharacteristicSpecification to have its
own set of CharacteristicSpecValue, although it is not recommended.

4.12.49.        Figure R.24 - CharacteristicSpecification for RootEntity RootEntityType

CharacteristicSpecifications can be shared by a number of RootEntityType instances. The “use” entity contains attributes that may be specific to a
use of the CharacteristicSpecification by an instance of a RootEntityType.

For example, a CharacteristicSpec with “bandwidth” value for the name attribute could be named “download speed” in one use and “upload speed” in
another use.

The values assigned to these attributes override the values assigned to an instance of a CharacteristicSpecification. For example, the minCardinality
attribute assigned to an instance of CharacteristicSpecification may be zero, indicating that a value does not have to be assigned for an instance of a
CharacteristicValue; however, for a particular use of the CharacteristicSpecification, the attribute must be assigned a value. In this case the
minCardinality attribute in the “use” entity would be assigned a value of one.

The package attribute, also a “use” entity attribute, indicates that the attribute represents a composite attribute, similar to the composite/atomic
pattern without the explicit sub-classing. When this attributes value is set to “true” a number of attributes do not apply, such as min and
maxCardinality, unique, canBeOverridden, and extensible.

The use of an attribute by a RootEntityType may also require a further restriction on the values that a Characteristic can be assigned. The
associations between CharacteristicSpecValue and the “use” entities can be used to define the further restriction. For example, the instances of
CharacteristicSpecValue for the instance of the “color” CharacteristicSpecification may contain the entire rainbow of colors, but only a subset of colors
is available for the cable modem.

Similarly, the two “SpecValueUse” entities enable different default values to be specified for the use of values associated with the use of a
CharacteristicSpecification by a RootEntityType.

CharacteristicSpecification can be related to specification entities in a number of ways. The relationship can be inherited if a specification entity is a
subclass of EntitySpecification or via a direct association between the CharacteristicSpecification and the specification entity.

4.12.51.         Figure A 1 - Instances of valid color values

More Details on Characteristics

As stated in the introduction, the CharacteristicSpecification/CharacteristicSpecValue pattern is intended to be used to dynamically define attributes
for any business entity. This pattern is very powerful but not easy to understand. Section 1.13 presents it to some level of details that may not be
sufficient to understand all aspects of its expressive power. In particular, Figure Ch.01 Characteristic Specification is very complex.

The purpose of this section is to try explain this pattern and the related artefacts in Figure Ch.01 in a simple and progressive way.

We will illustrate our presentation with the example of Cars and CarSpecifications. While it is deliberately away from the Telecom domain, we expect
that it is simple to understand, not controversial and general enough to be easily adaptable to our Telecom domain.

What does “ dynamically define attributes for any business entity” mean ?

The usual way to define specific attributes for a given business entity is to add these attributes in the class of the model that represents this entity.
Based on this modification of the model, the implementation of the corresponding class (data base or source code) must be modified, recompiled and
the corresponding run time need to be re-executed to take into consideration the modifications introduced.

The approach proposed by the CharacteristicSpecification/CharacteristicSpecValue pattern is, at run time, to create instances of
CharacteristicSpecification (or simply called characteristics) and instances of associated CharacteristicSpecValues.

Once this is done, there are two ways these characteristics can be used:

•     Either associate an instance of a CharacteristicSpecification to an instance of a business entity that need to be extended

•     Or use the RootEntityType meta approach. In this case, characteristics can be added to an instance of RootEntityType, meaning that these added
      characteristics will apply to the SID Entity (SID class) corresponding to the instance of the RootEntityType (remember that The RootEntityType in
      the SID is populated with a list of all business entities contained in the SID).
      Figure Ch.04 illustrates this approach with the SID Entity “Service” that is extended with two characteristics “LastStatusChange” and
      “StatusUpdatedBy”.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                               370 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



    It then means that any instance of this extended class will be extended with these two characteristics.
    From an implementation view point it can be interpreted as adding two new columns to the data base table representing this SID class.



Below we will further illustrate the first way.

Use Case 1:

Let’s assume that the model already contains a static class “CarSpec” inheriting from EntitySpec.

This class “CarSpec” will be used to create instances of models of cars that will populate a catalog (for example, the catalog of all the models of a
given constructor).

This class “CarSpec” has its own set of attributes that will characterize each car model instance, such as the possible energy, the possible engine
power and the possible colors, among many others.

Now let’s assume that for a given instance of CarSpec (representing the car model Toyota Yaris as an example) we want to differentiate the color of
the roof and the color of the body. The valid values for the roof or the body will be a subset of the valid values for the general Color characteristic that
is used in general for all car models of this constructor which we assume to be: Black, Blue, Green, Pink and Yellow.

The general Color characteristic is represented as an instance of CharacteristicSpecification aggregating a set of instances of
CharacteristicSpecValue representing the various valid colors.

color of the roof and another one for the color of the body. We will use instances of EntitySpecCharUse associated with instances of
EntitySpecCharValueUse for this purpose. See below.

4.12.56.        Figure R.26 - Characteristic Value

Following Figure depicts CharacteristicValues and their associations to Entities, in the figure a RootEntity. A CharacteristicValue is one chosen or
entered for an Entity for a specific CharacteristicSpecification. In the example above the instances would be “blue” for the face plate and black for the
case. The values would be related to the respective CharacteristicSpecValue.

When values are not specified in CharacteristicSpecValue or the other “spec value” entities shown in the figure, but are entered for a
CharacteristicSpecification, then CharacteristicValue is related to CharacteristicSpecification or one of the other “spec” entities shown in the figure.
For example, a startTime is a CharacteristicSpecification for a Video on Demand, an entered value of 6 Aug 2007 would be related to startTime. This
one of the two cases when a CharacteristicValue would be related to a CharacteristicSpecification and when the value attribute of CharacteristicValue
would have to be populated. The other case is when a value is chosen from a range of values defined by the CharacteristicSpecValue. When a
CharacteristicSpecValue is chosen from a list of discrete values or it is unchangeable (via the canBeOverridden attribute set to “false”), there is no
need to populate the value attribute, unless performance considerations required this from an implementation perspective. In these cases the
instance of CharacteristicValue is related to the instance of CharacteristicSpecValue and not the instance of CharacteristicSpecification.

Note: CharacteristicValue can also be directly related to an entity, such as ServicePerformance or Customer if they do not inherit from the RootEntity

4.12.59.        Figure R.29 - Content Characteristic Specs

Characteristic-Based Pricing and Charging

In the not-so-distant past factors influencing the price of a product were based on the usage of network resources and other resources, measured by
such factors as amount of time, or some other unit, a resource was used or the amount of bandwidth consumed. But once the usage of other types of
resources, such as content, is included, many other characteristics influence the price.

This model was originally envisioned to support content pricing and charging, but it can be used for any type of pricing and charging that is based on
characteristics and their values. As will be seen it can also be used for usage-based pricing d charging also.

Policy versus Simplicity

When rules for pricing (and charging) were first being developed in the Information Framework (SID) many years ago, Reliance on the policy model
was not total. An additional set of entities were under development to support simple rules, such as quantity-based pricing and tiered-based pricing.
However, this additional set was not introduced in the released version of the SID. Rather, all rules relied on policy. Many members believe that
using only policy is a bit extreme. A balance can be achieved between simplicity and complexity.

This balance was introduced into the SID in release 6.0 with the addition of an association between ProductSpecCharacteristicValue and
ProductOfferingPrice. This provided a simple rule that enabled a price to be dependent on value(s) of one or more characteristics.

This balance continued in SID in release 9.0 with the arrival of a new Level 2 ABE (Aggregate Business Entity, a cohesive group of entities) named
Pricing Logic Algorithm within the Product Offering Level 1 ABE. This ABE used Product Specification Characteristics to provide support for pricing
that involves parameters which are multi-dimensional matrices, in which each dimension is defined by the valid values for a specific characteristic.
For example, a PLA which rates events based on customer type, there is one instantiation in which customer type is either residential or commercial
(and in which case the size of the dimension of the matrix is 2) and in other instantiation the customer type can be student, normal and senior citizen
(in this case the size of the dimension is 3).

And, the use of a simple approach is used here.

•     The Characteristic Model
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              383 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     The generalized application of the CharacteristicSpecification/CharacteristicValue pattern will be used to demonstrate this.

•     The Pricing and Charging Scenario

A typical and simple scenario will be used to demonstrate the model. Pay per view Video on Demand (VoD) product depends on the format, standard
(STD) or high definition (HD) with a special price given for certain movies viewed in HD during a Saturday and Sunday period.

Shown in the following Figure is an Object Diagram that shows how the current model supports movies. There are other attributes associated with a
movie, such as run time, director, and so forth that are not shown in this example.




                                                           Figure R.29 - Content Characteristic Specs




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                 384 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.12.60.         Figure R.30 - Updated Characteristic Use Entities

The use of the EntitySpecification/Entity pattern was considered to support the requirements. However, this would proliferate the number of
EntitySpecifications and Entities from an implementation perspective. In this example, there would have to be a separate instance of
EntitySpecification for each movie and a separate instance of an Entity for each chosen movie. Using the generalized
CharacteristicSpecification/Characteristic model eliminates this. In this example there could be a single instance of an EntitySpecification that
represents Pay Per View and a single instance of and Entity that represents a Pay Per View subscription.

Enhancements to the Characteristic Model

•     Add two new entities to the characteristic model

•     Add associations to ProductOfferingPrice and ComponentProductPrice.

Also, catalogs of movies can be created by adding a composite CharacteristicSpecification named Video Catalog and associating the Movie Title
composite with it. Since this is dependent on the “use” of the values, a recursive association on EntitySpecCharValueUse is required. A similar
association and association class is also added to EntitySpecCharUse as shown in Figure Ch.06 – Updated Characteristic Use Entities.

4.12.62.         Figure R.32 - Content Charging Offer Price

Shown in the following Figure is an example where the high definition (HD) format for the movie 2012 has one price, while the same movie has a
different price for standard (STD) format.

The STD and HD values do not have to be repeated as instances of CharacteristicSpecValues. The Format instance of CharacteristicSpecification
can be shared among all movie titles. The names of the objects in the diagram are illustrative only. If the price varied only by HD and STD and not
by title then dependencies could be created that were only related to the format values.

If the pay per view price was the same for every movie, then there would be no need for this type of pricing. The current Product Offering to Product
Offering Price association would be support this. Also, the ProductOfferingPrice name is illustrative only; the price can be shared by multiple
CharacteristicPriceDependencies.

Note that an attribute may be required in Product Offering and/or Product Specification to indicate that choices can be made when accessing an
associated Product.

By relating price dependencies to the “use” entities:

•     A VoD subscription offering with unlimited free viewing with a fixed subscription price can be created by not associating use attributes with price
      dependencies and associating a ProductOfferingPrice to the VoD subscription offering

•     A VoD subscription offering with unlimited free viewing except for “special” movies can be created by associating only the specials uses with price
      dependencies and associating a ProductOfferingPrice to the VoD subscription offering

•     A VoD pay per view offering can be created by associating appropriate use attributes, such as title and format, with price dependencies.

4.12.66.         Figure R.36 – Content Charging Product Price

Shown in the following Figure is an example of viewing of the HD version of 2012, and the price that was determined based on the characteristic
values that represent the chosen movie.

Domain Options – Product and/or Resource

The changes is only made to the Characteristic Spec ABE. All other ABEs where this pattern is duplicated are not updated. By promoting the use
entities, any entity that does not inherit from the EntitySpecification, or is an entity (RootyEntityType) that does not have a specification, can be
related to the use entities.

The choice using this model in the Product and/or Resource domain may be based upon a company’s business model. In the content example
presented here, a company may treat each request for content as an order associated with a ProductOffering. Or, a company may treat each request
as some form of usage. Or a company may use a hybrid model that combines the treatment of requests.

This model supports all three of these business models, because it can be used for

•     Product Spec/Offer Pricing/Charging (as shown in the diagrams in this section)

•     Usage Pricing/Charging

•     Resource Spec Pricing/Charging.

One other factor needs to be considered when making the choice. The information may have to be duplicated because of application boundaries. A
CRM application may need this information as well as Provisioning and Inventory applications.

There are also some application/database implementation considerations

•     The use of one set of shared characteristic tables (if the applications are cooperative enough to do this!)

•     If the data is only contained in one application then other applications have to request characteristic data from it.

4.12.68.        Figure R.38 - Entity Meta-Model

Entire books have been written on meta-modeling. This section does not attempt to do complete justice to this topic. However, it does overview some
initial modeling thoughts on extending an implementation of the SID to include the dynamic addition of entities, meta-concepts (subclasses and
attributes currently supported by the SID), associations, and association classes.

A SID implementer can implement the models described in this section to rapidly adapt a database to changed information requirements without the
need to make substantial changes to the database. At some point in time, the meta-data instances may be converted to explicitly modeled and
implemented entities, subclasses, associations, association classes, and attributes.

Some SID implementers may view meta-modeling associations as more an implementation view than an information view of entities and their
associations. If the choice is that this is an implementation view then the model contained in this section can be placed in an implementation model
rather than an information model.

Note that Object (Instance) diagrams shown in this part of the guide book were not added to the SID model. They are included here for illustrative
purposes only.

Meta-modeling entities serve as the starting point that enables the dynamic addition of entities (classes and subclasses), eliminating the need to add
them explicitly to an implementation of the SID. These entities can then be extended via the dynamic addition of attributes and associations. The
existing SID RootEntityType, which has been enhanced to support this capability, is shown the following Figure.

4.12.69.        Figure As.01a – Root Entity Instances and Associations

Figure As.01a – Root Entity Instances and Associations shows an Object diagram that contains all the examples of RootEntityType instances
described to this point.

Note that in the figure, the Customer instance is an explicit subclass of PartyRole, but the instance of this association is not shown in the figure. Also,
this model can support the dynamic addition of entities and subclasses not contained in the SID model. Examples of this are not shown in the figure
either.

The meta-model is only part of the solution to support dynamic extensibility. Application functionality, including user interfaces, must also be
developed. A representative set of insights into functionality, excluding user interfaces, will be described next.

When creating/updating an instance of an EntitySpecification in a database, such as a CompoundResourceSpecAtomic, the application can ask if it is
to be dynamically extended. If the answer is yes, an instance of RootEntityType is created. Once the extensions have been created/updated, then the
value of the attribute “dynamicAttrAssoc” can be set to indicate whether attributes or associations or both have been added. The database for the
meta-model, including implementation considerations for it, will be shown later after all meta-modeling concepts have been described.

When adding an instance of a specification, the application could ask if a corresponding entity should be created. Adding the corresponding entity will
enable the creation of dynamic associations between the entity and other entities. This follows from the fact that an association between
specifications is often mirrored for the entities described by the specification. This will also assist the application to automatically create instances of
RootEntity, which may participate in instances of the associations.

4.12.70.        Figure As.02a – Properties of an Association

Meta-Modeling – Existing Support for Subclasses and Attributes

The dynamic addition of attributes and subclasses is already supported in the SID via two patterns, EntitySpecification/Entity and the
CharacteristicSpecification/CharacteristicValue, sometimes referred to as the Dynamic Object pattern. They will be briefly revisited in the section
Error: Reference source not found in this part of the guide book, where inheriting attributes (and associations) will be described.

The use of these two SID patterns is also often referred to as dynamic sub-classing. But what does this mean? It means that a subclass of the
applicable EntitySpecification and/or a subclass of the applicable Entity are implied, rather than explicitly modeled. The and/or is based on the
instances of CharacteristicSpecifications related to the instance of the EntitySpecification.

If all the CharacteristicSpecValue instances are fixed (cannot be configured), then there is only an implied subclass of the EntitySpecification. If all the
CharacteristicSpecValue instances are configurable, then there is only an implied subclass of the Entity. If the CharacteristicSpecValue instances are
fixed and configurable, then there are implied subclasses of the EntitySpecification and the Entity.

An application could provide the capability (user interface and logic) to add instances of CharacteristicValue when adding an instance of a RootEntity.
This capability would determine if the RootEntityType that describes the RootEntity has any related instances of CharacteristicSpecification. The
dynamicAttrAssoc attribute of RootEntityType would be used to determine this.

Implementing the meta-model to support the dynamic addition of classes and subclasses, both implicit and explicit, means that inheriting attributes
must be supported. Existing SID entities should be handled by transformation of the information model to the data model by rolling down the super-
classes. A representative set of application logic that supports inheritance for dynamically added classes and subclasses is described next.

Application logic can be employed to support inheriting attributes by copying instances of CharacteristicSpecifications and CharacteristicSpecValues
to subclasses for dynamically added classes and subclasses, both implicit and explicit. The application would create instances of
RootEntityTypeCharUse and RootEntityTypeCharValueUse. If copying, rather than true inheritance, is done, then the ability to change the properties
(attributes) of the Characteristic entities of the subclasses should be prevented by the application to avoid inconsistencies. The application could also
ask if selected instances of RootEntityTypeCharValueUse should also be created.


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                 400 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



For those SID implementer’s who decide to use a meta-model like the one described here, there is an important point to keep in mind. Existing
associations between entities that represent specifications and applications of the characteristics or directly to characteristics are no longer
necessary. The section Error: Reference source not found in part of the guide book will further explain this.



Meta-Modeling Associations

Requirements for meta-modeling associations, like those for entities and attributes, can be gathered by looking at the properties of an association and
its roles in a UML tool. Figure As.02a – Properties of an Association shows properties of an association.

4.12.71.        Figure R.39 - Association Specification Meta-Model

The following Figure shows the meta-model based on a subset of these requirements.

The AssociationSpecification “validFor” attribute was intentionally left without a data type to make implementation in a database simpler. Also, the
multiplicity of the “AssociationSpecificationDefinedBy” multiplicity for the “AssociationRoleSpecification” role was set to 2. This only permits binary
(two entities involved) associations.

The requirements could be used to identify more attributes that further define the association entities. In fact, there are more entities that could be
added to the model to support stereotypes, constraints, and so forth, as shown in the following Figure. That is why the introduction to these meta-
modeling sections mentioned that the models/thoughts described here are just initial.

4.12.76.        Figure R.41 - Association Class Specification Meta-Model

The specification of this association is simple, because it assumes no history when an instance of a Base Station Controller’s association
(SupportedBy) to a Mobile Switching Center is changed from one instance of a Mobile Switching Center to another. Otherwise the specification for the
association would be an *-*, which means the specification for an association class (entity) should be introduced into the meta-model, as shown in
Figure As.04 – Association Class Specification.

The model shown in the figure provides the capability to define a specification for an association class or not. The 0,1 multiplicity from
AssociationSpecification to RootEntityType defines this capability.

It should be kept in mind that another meta-modeling option is to not add the association between AssociationSpecification and RootEntityType. The
equivalent support could be provided by adding an instance of RootEntityType that represents a class, rather than an association class. This
instance would be related to the two instances of the RootEntityTypes involved in the *-* association by creating two instances of
AssocationSpecification. In modeling, this represent what is often referred to as an intersection or link entity, which represents the resolution of a *-*
association.

The association meta-model is only part of the solution to support the dynamic addition of associations. Application functionality, including user
interfaces, must also be developed. A representative set of insights into functionality, excluding user interface details, will be described next.

An application could provide the capability (user interface and logic) to add instances of associations when adding an instance of a RootEntity. This
capability would determine if the RootEntityType that describes the RootEntity has any related instances of AssociationSpecification. The
dynamicAttrAssoc attribute of RootEntityType would be used to determine this.

Implementing the meta-model to support the dynamic addition of classes and subclasses, both implicit and explicit, means that inheriting associations
must be supported. For existing SID entities, this should be handled by transformation of the information model to the data model by rolling down the
super-classes.

Application logic can be employed to support inheriting associations by copying instances of AssociationSpecifications and
AssociationRoleSpecifications to subclasses for dynamically added classes and subclasses, both implicit and explicit. If copying, rather than true
inheritance, is provided, then the ability to change the properties (attributes) of the specifications (Association and AssociationRole) of the subclasses

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                407 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



should be prevented by the application to avoid inconsistencies. When an instance of an entity is created/updated the application can ask if an
instance of an association must/may be added/updated.

4.12.77.        Figure As.04a – UML Association Implementation Model

Meta-Modeling Behavior

The last component of the meta-model is the model for behavior, or operations/methods. This is beyond the scope of what is described here, as there
are quite a few variations on how this could be modelled. And, any model presented here may not be a suitable representation in many cases.
However, a starting point would be to use the properties of operations in the UML model as a set of requirements.

Besides these, there would also have to be provisions in the meta-model on how to invoke the behavior. One other implementation consideration is
that there should be a separate library for the code, since this code would typically be developed outside of a solution. For example, if a solution
vendor provides this capability as part of a solution, the responsibility for this additional code should not be theirs.

The Meta-Model Database

The meta-model database described in this section represents a simplification of the UML model. It was designed with performance considerations in
mind. There are certainly other implementations of the meta-model that may or may not perform better.

The model of the database supporting the UML model includes two associations that can be added to the information model or the physical data
model. The model that is shown here is based on associations added to the UML model shown in Figure As.04a – UML Association Implementation
Model.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                            409 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Figure As.04a – UML Association Implementation Model




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                410 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.12.78.        Figure As.04b – Association Meta-Model Logical Data Model

An association has been added between the association role entities and their related root entities. Each association entity now references the entity
that represents the role AND the related entity. This should improve performance by providing the ability to access both entities involved in an
association in a single database record (row). For example, consider the instance of the Base Station Controller AssociationRoleSpecification; this
role is an AssociationRoleSpecification instance for the Mobile Switching Center “supports” Base Station Controller instance of
AssociationSpecification; this instance of AssociationRoleSpecification would include the foreign key referencing the Base Station Controller
RootEntityType instance, as well as secondary reference to the Mobile Switching Center RootEntityType instance. The associations were added to
the UML model so that the applicable foreign keys would be automatically generated.

Figure As.04b – Association Meta-Model Logical Data Model shows the logical data model with the associations labeled primary and secondary to
reflect this.




                                               Figure As.04b – Association Meta-Model Logical Data Model

This model, combined with the database implementation of the Characteristic model, and its entities’ associations with RootEntityType and
RootEntity, can represent the complete meta-model. The full model is show in Figure As.04c Full Meta-Model Including Characteristics.




                                                Figure As.04c Full Meta-Model Including Characteristics




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                          412 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.12.80.        Figure As.04d – Association Meta-Model Physical Database

In the section on meta-modeling subclasses and attributes, an important point was made for SID implementer’s who choose to take this approach. It
is no longer necessary to use the repeated applications of the Characteristic pattern in the existing SID model or directly relating existing SID entities
to characteristic “use” entities. This is demonstrated in Figure As.04d – Full Meta-Model Including Characteristics. For example, the Base Station
Controller instance of CompoundResourceSpecification can be stored as an instance of RootEntityType; instances of CharacteristicSpecifications can
be related to it by creating instances of RootEntityTypeCharUse.

Figure As.04d – Association Meta-Model Physical Database shows the database.

The transformation from the information model to the data model rolled down the two association specification entities. Therefore they are not
included in the data model, but their attributes are. An additional database consideration is that an index could be added on the “name” attribute. This
would provide a way to search for association specifications for an instance of a RootEntityType. If necessary, an index on “name” could also be
added to the AssociationRole table, if the “name” column from the AssocationRoleSpecification table is added to it.

A non-unique index on the “name” attribute of RootEntityType could be added. By doing this, instances of RootEntityType can be easily accessed
when adding new attributes and/or associations to it.

An attribute most likely also should be added to RootEntityType, and possibly RootEntity, and would indicate if an instance is a “true” dynamic entity.
These entities are not contained in the database generated from SID entities or that contains implied subclasses in the database. The attribute(s)
could be added to the information model, rather than the data model. The addition would make finding instances of these dynamically added entities
easier, particularly when dealing with (adding/updating/deleting/retrieving) instances of RootEntity.

The keys of the Root entities should be stored in instances of SID entities. This would provide an indication that the entities have been extended. The
keys of both RootEntityType and RootEntity should be stored in instances of SID implied subclasses. This is done because both the implied subclass
and its superclass may have both been dynamically extended. For example, the ProductSpecification entity itself may have been dynamically
extended, from which the implied subclasses of it inherit.

The meta-model database can be deployed by application or domain to avoid any possible performance issues. However, dynamically added cross
domain associations need to be considered when doing this.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                               413 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



One final point about implementing a meta-model is that dynamically added entities and their subtypes, associations, and attributes should be
considered for explicit modeling at some point in time. Their stability should be a consideration for determining when this should be done.

Attachment represents a group of classes that support an attachment like image, video and document for any RootEntity. The following Figure shows

Attachment

This is the class to represent an attachment like document, image or video to any RootEntity or other Business Entity in the model.

This class is concrete and it can be used without a sub-class. However, one can extend this class to add more specific behavior of an entity
attachment. The AttachmentDocumentation and AttachmentImage are examples of such extension.

AttachmentDocumentation

This is the extended class representing a documentation attachment.

AttachmentImage

This is the extended class representing an image attachment.

4.12.83.        Figure R.44 - External System Capability

External System Capability represents a logical grouping of capabilities that exposed by one system to another system. For example, a catalog
modeler can build a product or service by composing these capabilities. The following Example demonstrates how these capabilities can be used
together to build a location API product. These group of logical capabilities are representing a system capability model as seen by its interfaces for
given capability so they can facilitate a catalog-driven integration approach. For example, what charging capabilities offered by an online charging
system, or what policy model supported by a PCRF system.

ExternalSystemCapability

This is an abstract class, and is the root of the External system capabilities. It extends EntitySpecification to define common attributes, relationships
and behavior that all external system capability subclasses use and take part in.

The ExternalSystemCapability model has four main purposes:

To represent different types of capability,

To represent which system offers the capability

To represent how different entity specification like PS, CFSS, RFSS can use these capabilities and

To represent (optionally) how these capabilities can be accessed by a system like an ordering system thru one or more APIs

There are many different types of capabilities. A system like a charging system can offer multiple capabilities like pre-paid and post-paid charging and
a capability can be offered by multiple external system instances.

The following Figure shows the UML diagram of the objects in this fragment.

FulfilmentCapabilitySpecification

This is the class representing a fulfilment capability specification as supported by a fulfilment/ordering system

This is the class representing a charging capability specification as supported by a charging system like OCS.

BillingCapabilitySpecification

This is the class representing a billing capability specification as supported by a billing system.

PolicyCapabilitySpecification

This is the class representing a policy capability specification as supported by a policy management system like PCRF.

AssuranceCapabilitySpecification

This is the class representing an assurance capability specification as supported by an assurance system.

4.12.84.        Association ABE

A pattern that enables associations to be dynamically created between explicitly modeled SID entities (classes and subclasses) as well as implicit
subclasses created using the EntitySpecification/Entity pattern.

4.12.85.        Characteristic ABE

A pattern (the content of the ABE) enables attributes unique to a type of entity to be created via its association to EntitySpecification and enables
attributes unique to a certain entity to be created via its association to RootEntityType. A RootEntityType is an entity in the SID. For example,
characteristics could be defined for entities (instances of RootEntityType) such as Product, Customer, and ProductSpecification that apply to all
instances of those entities.

4.12.85.1.      Figure Char.01 - Characteristic ABE Related Entities

Following are the business entities aggregated under the Characteristic Aggregate Business Entity.

4.12.90.        Root Entity Group ABE

The Root Entity Group ABE enables groups of Root Entities to be related as an alternate to using the RootEntityRelationship entity in order to reduce
the number of instances of RootEntityRelationship. It also enables choices of Root Entities to be made from the group. For example, choose 3 out of
7 channel packs. And, to define expressions that constrain Root Entity members of a group. For example, either channel pack 3 or channel pack 4
can be chosen, but not both.

4.13. Account ABE
The Account ABE contains all entities needed for specifying basic attributes and relationships that describe an account.

4.13.1.         Figure Acc.01 - Account Overview

An Account specifies basic attributes and relationships that describe an account.

An Account aims to register all Credits and Debits according to criteria. It carries one or many Balances (results of Credits and Debits) and might use
one or many Currencies.

Each Account is possessed by one or many PartyRoles such as the Holder or the Payer of a CustomerBillingAccount.

Each Account might be related to other Accounts such as a parent / child relationship.

BusinessPartnerAccount, CustomerBillingAccount and FinancialAccount are types of Account.

A BusinessPartnerAccount and CustomerBillingAccount carries additional information (such as creditLimit) and have specific relationships.

A FinancialAccount is part of the sub-ledger accounting that aggregates the amounts of one or more business partner and / or Customer accounts
owned by a given party. It’s an internal view of the CSP to manage incomes and outcomes.

Each accounting event from a BusinessPartnerAccount or CustomerBillingAccount is logged to a FinancialAccount according to the assignment rules
specified by BsuinessPartnerAccountAssignment and CustomerBillingAccountAssignment.
