4.19.13.        Figure Me.07a - Metric Threshold Definition - Derived Associations

The following Figure shows an overview of how threshold are specified for a MetricDefMeasure.

A MetricDefMeasure specifies a measure of a specific aspect of an entity, such as lost packets or dealer sales target achievement, defined for a
MetricDefinition that may trigger consequence (s) (MetricDefMeasureConsequence) if a threshold (MetricDefMeasureThreshold) is crossed.

A MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet provides a way to group a set of one or more MetricDefMeasureThresholdRules specifying the condition to
evaluate for applying consequences.

The thresholdType specifies the type of MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule contained in the MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet.

The value is “LogicDefinition” if the MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet contains only MetricThresholdRuleDefinition instances and “PreDefined” if it
contains only PreDefinedMetricThresholdRule instances.

A MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet is used to evaluate one or more MetricDefMesures.

Threshold rules (MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule) may differ in the condition that they apply, a raise condition or a clear condition, and in the severity
of the threshold crossing.

A MetricDefMeasureConsequence defines the action (prescribed action or notification) taken when a MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule is crossed and
might depend on MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule applied or not for the previous MetricMeasure..

When a MetricDefMeasureConsequence is applied (track as a MetricMeasureConsequence) this is the result of a single rule
(MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule) being crossed.

Different consequences (MetricDefMeasureConsequence) can be defined for different severities.

The repeatAction attribute on MetricDefMeasureConsequence is an indicator used to specify that a consequence

     •   should cease being applied if a value is in the same range as the previous value

     •   or continue being applied if a value is in the same range as the previous value.
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                             541 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



If the repeatApplication is True, the consequence is always applied as soon as the MetricMeasure value is in the range of values and if the
repeatApplication is False, the consequence is applied only if the previous MetricMeasure value wasn’t in the same range.

Nota:

     •   The two associations pre-fixed by a slash correspond to derived associations.

     •   A derived association represents synthetically several associations. See example with the next figure.

                                         +   ID: String                                                                                                                   MetricDefinition

                                         +   repeatAction: Boolean
                                         +   validFor: TimePeriod                     1..*

The following Figure shows the detailed associations for MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule.

The MetricDefMeasureThreshConsDependency specifies the type of dependency that may exist between MetricDefMeasureConsequence and
MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule. For example, "the consequence is triggered only if the rule has been applied previously", or "if the rule has already
been applied then do not trigger the consequence".

The two associations MetricDefMeasureConsequenceDependentUpon, MetricDefMeasureThresholdRuleDefines and the entity
MetricDefMeasureThreshConsDependency are summarized through the derived association “/MetricDefMeasureConsequenceDependsOn” on the

The MetricDefMeasureThresholdConsequence specifies the sequence of application when several MetricDefMeasureConsequence have to be
applied for a specific MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule.

The two associations MetricDefMeasureThresholdRuleResultsIn, MetricDefMeasureConsequenceDeterminedBy and the entity
MetricDefMeasureThresholdConsequence are summarized through the derived association “/MetricDefMeasureThresholdRuleResultsIn” on the

4.19.16.        Figure Me.08 - Metric Definition Measure Threshold Rule

The following Figure shows the different types of threshold rules that might be defined.

A MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule is a rule that defines the condition (raise or clear) to achieve to apply consequences when a threshold
(MetricDefMeasureThreshold) is crossed or ceased to be crossed. It may behave in one of two different ways:

     •   Either it provides attributes for various kinds of threshold logic definition (MetricThresholdRuleDefinition),

    • or it is defined by a derivation algorithm that represents a predefined logic that can be executed with parameters
(PreDefinedMetricThresholdRule).

A MetricThresholdRuleDefinition specifies the threshold range of values (lower and upper conformance target) with comparators (lower and upper
comparator), tolerances and, grace periods, and a comparator.

Each comparator is an operator that specifies whether MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet is crossed when it is above or below a conformance target.
For example, a threshold may specify that when average lost packets indicator exceeds a value of 2000 and is under 3000, then the threshold is
crossed.

A PreDefinedMetricThresholdRule is a "blackbox" logic and is defined by a named predefined logic that can be executed with parameters. Therefore
the PreDefinedMetricThresholdRule entity will hold the rule name, and the PreDefinedMetricThresholdRuleParam will define the parameters that are
required for executing the PreDefinedMetricThresholdRule. As these parameters are often indicator specifications, they can be associated to the
MetricDefMeasure entity.

4.19.17.           Figure Me.07-08-I01 – Simple consequence

The following examples illustrates the use of MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet, MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet and MetricDefMeasureConsequence.

A Metric is defined to evaluate the “Average Layer 3 packet roundtrip delay in millisecond”

•     5 value ranges (thresholds) in millisecond are defined for that Metric

•     [0;100[     Good

•     [100;250[     Acceptable

•     [250;1000[     Bad

•     [1000;+¥[     Very Bad

•     nil value Not measurable
      (may be due to problems such as disconnect or due to sensor failure)

•     for each value range one or more Consequences to trigger might be defined:

•     [0;100[     Green GUI

•     [100;250[     Green GUI

•     [250;1000[     Light Orange GUI & raise a Warning notification

•     [1000;+¥[     Dark Orange GUI & raise a Major notification

•     nil value    Red GUI & trigger the diagnose of “not measurable”

•     •   Some consequences are defined according to the previous measure’s value range
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                  548 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     no longer [0;100[ and whatever the new value is   notify degradation

•     no longer [1000;+¥[ and new value [0;250[   notify end of Major trouble

Rk: To have more simple diagrams the entities used to detail the relationships are not represented (MetricDefMeasureThreshConsDependency and
MetricDefMeasureThresholdConsequence) in the illustrations.

Here for any measure in the range of [0;100[ the consequence “update GUI to green” is triggered.

According to the previous measure (MetricMeasure) compared to the new measure, a consequence might be triggered.

In this example, if the previous measure was between 0 to 100 and the new measure is out of this range of values (specified by the
“DependsOnPrevious” association), then a notification is sent to alert about the degradation.

4.19.20.        Figure Me.07-08-I04 – MetricThresholdRuleDef tolerancePeriod, toleranceNumber

In this example, we assume that the captureFrequency defined by the MetricDefMeasure is 10 seconds. So, we will evaluate 7 measures during 60
seconds.

The tolerancePeriod is 60 seconds and the numberOfAllowedCrossing is 1.

Then if we evaluate the following measures 50, 500, 2000, 100, 60, 80, 90

no consequence will be initiated.

But if the measures are the following 50, 500, 2000, 100, 60, 80, 1500 then the two consequences will be initiated.

Note: When no value is specified for a conformanceTarget Upper or Lower this means it is boundless.

4.19.22.         Figure Me.07-08-I06 – Example 2 of rules for a MetricDefMeasure

Other example for a Metric defined to evaluate the “Average Layer 3 packet roundtrip delay in millisecond”

Exceptions (thresholds) in millisecond are defined for that Metric

•     Normal, 0 =< value < 500, no need to be modeled, as only exceptions are modeled

•     Major, Crosses: 500 =< value, Clear exception: value < 460

•     nil value Not measurable
      (may be due to problems such as disconnect or due to sensor failure)

For each event of a threshold crossing/ceased to cross one or more Consequences to trigger might be defined:

•     Raise an Alarm of the specific severity

•     Clear an Alarm of the specific severity

4.19.24.        Figure Me.09 - Metric Measure Notification definition

Different kinds of consequences may be applied as a consequence of threshold. The following Figure shows how to describe notification
consequences.

A MetricDefMeasureConsequence either results in a prescribed action (prescribedAction) or one or more ConsequenceMetricNotificationSpecs.

If a MetricMeasureConsequence is triggered by a MetricMeasure (through a MetricMeasureThresholfRule) according to a
MetricDefMeasureConsequence and the MetricDefMeasureConsequence results in ConsequenceMetricNotificationSpecs, then a
ConsequenceMetricNotification is created for each of them.

A ConsequenceMetricConsequence is a type of ConsequenceMetricNotification, which in turn is a type of Notification.

A ConsequenceMetricNotification is described by a ConsequenceMetricNotificationSpec.

Note: To be consistent with the pattern Specifcation used for ConsequenceMetricNotificationSpec / ConsequenceMetricNotification, a
NotificationSpecification should exist in the Business Interaction ABE. This concept would specify the content pattern for each corresponding
notification.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                            559 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                                                                                                                                                             Notification

4.19.25.        Figure Me.10 - Metric Measure Definition Groups

The following Figure shows how MetricDefMeasures and MetricMeasures may be grouped when needed.

A MetricDefMeasureGroup is a set of MetricDefMeasures, usually reported in the same message by the equipment or the EMS.

A MetricMeasureGroup is a set of MetricMeasures, usually reported in the same message by the equipment or the EMS.

For example, a server will typically report CPU related metrics in a single message. These metrics may include: Maximum CPU usage, Average CPU
usage, System CPU usage and User CPU usage.

Mobile RAN may group related metrics by functional aspects in a technology context such as: LTE Intra-frequency handovers, UMTS Radio Access
or NodeB Utilization.

Standard protocols like MTOSI or 3GPP will pack the Metric Group in a single message either the same line in a CSV file or within XML tags when
the data format is XML.

4.19.26.        Figure Me.11 - Metric Measurement Job

The following Figure shows how to define jobs to measure MetricDefMeasures and MetricDefMeasureGroups.

A MetricManagementJob is a management activity that may consist of several steps but is performed as a single logical unit in a Metric context.

It can be a one-time activity or a regular one. In the latter case, a schedule definition is attached to it.

A MetricMeasurementJob always defines a scope of monitored objects using the MonitoredMetricObjectCriteria.

A MetricDefMeasure as well as a MetricDefMeasureGroup may be measured by one or more MetricMeasurementJob.

4.19.27.        Figure Me.12 - Monitored Metric Object Criterion

The following Figure shows the different types of MonitoredMetricObjectCriterion.

A MonitoredMetricObjectCriterion defines the scope of monitored objects for which a MeasurementJob is executed.

The scope is either defined:

     •   by a filter on a Class (sub-class MonitoredMetricClassCriterion)

     •   or by a list of instances (sub-class MonitoredMetricInstanceCriterion).

A MonitoredMetricClassCriterion is a type of MonitoredMetricObjectCriterion.

It defines the scope of monitored objects for which a MeasurementJob is executed.by a filter on a class through the relationship with RootEntityType.

A MonitoredMetricInstanceCriterion is a type of MonitoredMetricObjectCriterion.

It defines the scope of monitored objects for which a MeasurementJob is executed by a list of instances through the relationship with one or several
RootEntity instances.

4.19.28.        Figure Me.13 - Metric Threshold Job and MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet

The following Figure shows the different types of MetricMeasurementJob.

A MetricMeasurementJob may be

     •   either a job for evaluating thresholds defined over MetricDefMeasure (MetricMeasurementThresholdJob),

     •   a job for producing MetricMesures according to a MetricDefMeasure (MetricMeasurementProductionJob),

     •   a job for collecting MetricMeasures produced by another job (MetricMeasurementCollectionJob),

     •   or a job for collecting MetricMeasures produced by another job to make available MetricMeasures for subscribers. (AdhocMetricCollection).

A MetricMeasurementThresholdJob evaluates one MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet.

A MetricMeasurementJob defines a scope of monitored objects using the MonitoredMetricObjectCriteria.

A MetricMeasurementJob also defines a scope of monitored objects using the MonitoredMetricClassCriteria.

4.19.29.        Figure Me.14 - Metric Applicability

A MetricDefMeasure, MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule or even MetricDefMeasureConsequence might applicable only during certain periods.

In this case, the MetricDefMeasureApplicability is used to specify in detail each applicable period.

The MetricDefMeasureApplicability represents the time of day or days during which a MetricDefMeasure, MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule or even
MetricDefMeasureConsequence are measurable or not.

The applicableCode attribute Indicates whether an instance represents applicability or un-applicability.

4.19.31.        Figure Me.16 - Basic Metric Measure Entities

Once metrics are defined the values associated with them can be captured. Figure Me.21 – Metric shows the MetricMeasure entity and its directly
related entities.

The MetricMeasure might be collected for 0..1 RootEntity because some entities may be explicitly related to a MetricMeasure, such as those entities
currently in the Performance, including Service and Resource, and SLA entities, including ProductOffering and ServiceSpecification. These explicit
associations represent a more detailed association than the more general association to RootEntity.

This diagram uses RootEntity as an abstract concept showing how any entity can be associated to MetricMeasure.

Since RootEntity is also a real class in the SID model and is the base class of several ABEs (Resource, ResourceSpec, Service, ServiceSpec and
Policy) this diagram also includes examples on how to model Metrics that measure entities which are not subclasses of RootEntity in the SID model.

For example, suppose there is a need to collect a metric for the Service instance of RootEntityType. The first step is to select one of the
MetricDefinition(s) for it, then the MetricDefMeasure to use, then the MetricDefinitionDeterminationMethod.

The MetricMeasure is associated to the method and to RootEntity (not RootEntityType) that represents for example the instance of Service for which
the MetricMeasure is being collected.

There are indirect associations (i.e. derived) from the MetricMeasure to the MetricDefMeasure via the association to
MetricDefinitionDeterminationMethod and from MetricMeasure to MetricDefinition via its association to MetricDefMeasure.

4.19.32.        Figure Me.17 - Metric Threshold

A MetricDefMeasure specifies a measure of a specific aspect of an entity, such as lost packets or dealer sales target achievement, defined for a
MetricDefinition that may trigger consequence (s) (MetricDefMeasureConsequence) if a threshold (MetricDefMeasureThreshold) is crossed.

A MetricMeasure is a numerical value or text determined for a MetricDefMeasure that may be collected for a RootEntity, such as a connection or a
dealer.

A MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet provides a way to group a set of one or more MetricDefMeasureThresholdRules specifying the condition to
evaluate for applying consequences.

A MetricDefMeasureThresholdSet is used to evaluate one or more MetricDefMesures.

Threshold rules (MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule) may differ in the condition that they apply, a raise condition or a clear condition, and in the severity
of the threshold crossing.

A MetricDefMeasureConsequence defines the action taken when a MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule is crossed.

Different consequences (MetricDefMeasureConsequence) can be defined for different severities.

A MetricMeasureThresholdRule specifies the MetricDefMeasureThresholdRule whose threshold has been crossed by a MetricMeasure.

It triggers one or more MetricMeasureConsequences.For example, the prescribed action by a consequence might be to monitor the MetricMeasure
more closely or to notify a specific person or the lost packets MetricMeasure with a value of 2500 would be associated with the threshold of no more
than 2000 lost packets through a MetricMeasureThresholdRule.

4.19.37.        Figure Me.18 - Metric Observation

A MetricMeasurementJob might be triggered by the schedule or might be triggered by a request from a PartyRole.

In both cases, a MetricMeasurementObservation tracks each measurement (i.e. execution of a MetricMeasurementJob).

A MetricMeasurementObservation represents the contextual information of a measurement such as the observation period, the
MetricMeasurementJob used, the requester of the observation (PartyRole) and the scope of the observation through the related
MonitoredMetricObjectCriteria.

The MonitoredMetricObjectCriteria related to MetricMeasurementObservation has to be equal to or a sub-set of the one related to the concerned
MetricMeasurementJob.

A MetricMeasurementObservation also specifies the MectricMeasures resulting from this observation.

4.19.39.          Metric Best Practices

Metric Best Practices

Case 1 – TM Forum GB935 Business Metrics

Basic Metric Concepts

The business metrics that have been developed and housed in the Business Metrics Scorecard represent areas of business operation that are
important in assessing business performance, customer satisfaction/loyalty and operational efficiency. Any metric managed by the BMS team will be
called a business metric. A business metric must:

•     Be intelligible to anyone familiar with high-level telecom business concepts; i.e. a business metric does not require specialist or technical
      knowledge

•     Be appropriate to include in a business scorecard, i.e. a set of enterprise-level performance indicators of the business

•     Be part of a select minority of all metrics; i.e. it is not likely that all measurable things are business metrics.

According to the US National Institute of Standards and Technology, “a measure is for more concrete or objective attributes and metric for more
abstract, higher-level, or somewhat subjective attributes”. A measure is a more general concept than a metric. Any quantity can be measured, but a
good business metric also has a clearly understood preferred progression, a numerical directionality which indicates how we’d like to see the metric
change as the business improves. Preferred progression can come in many different flavors, like:

•       The higher, the better (like revenue)

•       The lower, the better (like customer response time)

•       The closer to a specific value, the better (like body temperature)

•       Can be equal to or lower than a value, but must not exceed (like a speed limit)

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                  581 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•       Can be equal to or higher than a specific value, but must not be lower (like bank account balance).

A metric is more likely to be some sort of comparison between two values, often expressed as a ratio, less frequently as a difference. But still, not all
metrics need be comparisons. So, for example, one can have a metric which is the number of days it takes to go from bill cycle close to bills issued.
However, even this simple count does have directionality – since the shorter this delay, the better.

A metric begins with the thing being measured. The measurand is the “entity quantified by the measure”. Important kinds of measurands in the BMS
work include:

•     number of subjects, or more specifically:

•     number of things (like # bills)

•     number of events (like # customer contacts)

•     number of people (like # customers)

•     monetary value of subjects

•     monetary value of things (like $ value of bills)

•     monetary value of processes or capabilities (like $ cost of assurance)

•     time durations

•     time durations for work effort (like # hours for all order processing)

•     time durations between events (like # hours between customer requested date and customer committed date).

A benchmark generally suggests that a number or set of numbers is available for the metric that is indicative of either an average or a target for the
industry. While the BMS team is responsible for defining business metrics, the Benchmarking team is responsible for running benchmarking studies
using these metrics, which generates reports containing benchmark numbers, both average and 80-percentile values, among others.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                582 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



A dimension is a breakdown scheme for a metric. For example, a metric like “Revenue” can be dimensioned by region, by service, by market
segment, etc. or indeed by any combination of these. Ideally, a dimension should not be part of a metric name, since one can instantiate many
metrics from one metric by running through all combinations of dimensions – and that just leads to clutter.

Note that Frameworx business metrics are service offering (SO) agnostic, and as such can be dimensioned by any appropriate service. That means
that the same metric (F-CE-2a Mean Duration to Fulfill) would be used to study service fulfillment time for residential broadband, mobile services, or
IP VPNs. Obviously, the data collected would be quite different. Thus, it is generally not appropriate, for example, to compare a fulfillment metric for
mobile service against a fulfillment benchmark for DSL service.

Business Metric Naming

A given metric might be expressed in multiple equivalent ways. For example, one could write “A as a % of B” or “% A of B” or “A/B %”. All this variety
can lead to the same metric erroneously entered multiple times in different namings, or else might make it difficult to find a metric given different
namings. So, in order to ensure consistency as well as concision in the naming of metrics, a set of naming types was used to standardize metrics,
detailed below.

What principles guided these metric name types? A good metric name should provide the gist of the metric without having to resort to looking up
further detail. For example, “# hours to fulfill service order” is better than “time to fulfill service order” since we do not need to look up the units. Also,
“# hours” is self-explanatory, so we do not need to write “Number of hours”. Also, business objects and their states have been standardized as much
as possible; so, we use “bills” instead of “invoices”, for example. Lists of standardize business objects, value drivers, and other expressions are
presented later in this chapter.

Some ratios are ratios of the same thing, where some subset based on attribute is in the numerator. This can be written very compactly. For example,
“% bills issued electronically” means “% of bills that were issued electronically out of total bills”. A percentage of two different things in the same units
can be written as “% opex, of revenue”, where the comma and “of” are used to indicate what the percent is based on. In the most general case, we
have two different things in a ratio, like cost of fulfillment per employee. Such ratios are generally not written as percent.

Prose metric names are written in title case. In addition to a Prose Name, a metric also has an “XLS Name”. This is driven by the need to have clear
formulas for derived metrics. So, while older formulas exist in Prose Formula form, we have made sure that every formula can also be expressed an
XLS Formula using the XLS Names.XLS Names are written in lowercase using the syntax for named ranges in Microsoft Excel1, or in other
spreadsheet products that adopt the same naming conventions.
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                     583 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



To summarize, metric naming in BMS FX13 has been driven by several needs:

•     To keep the name as short as possible.

•     To keep the name meaningful enough so that we reduce the need to reference the more complete definition in the documentation

•     To keep the structure of the names consistent

•     To keep the terms in the name consistent (see Business Object Taxonomy for BMS Measurand)

Below are the principle metric naming types used in Fx13:

Type 1: Count – Basic

•     Format: # <business-objects>

•     Example: # Bills

•     Format: # <business-objects><qualification>

•     Example: # Bills Issued

•     Note: It is important to follow the metric format exactly. Thus, it is “# Bills”, not “# of Bills”, not “# Bills Total”, not “Number of Bills”, etc.

Type 2: Valuation

•     Since currency is a count of dollars, it is similar to a count, except that the business object is not countable, but rather a financial concept that can
      be “evaluated”.

•     Format: $ <financial-object>

•     Example: $ Revenue


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                            584 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     Note: A native financial concept (like cost or revenue) is easily understood to be evaluable. But if the item being evaluated is not a native financial
      concept, like the money value of bills issued, we precede the object with “value of”. Thus bills, which can either be counted or evaluated, require
      the expression “$ Value of Bills” to clearly indicate we mean the dollar value and not the count of bills.

Type 3: Ratio – Simple

•     Format: % <business-object><qualification>

•     Implication: % <business-object><qualification> / <business-object> Total

•     Example: % Activation Failed

•     Implication: % Activations Failed / Activations Total

•     Note: This is the simplest kind of ratio to express since the same business object is referenced in the numerator and the denominator. Thus a %
      can be used to denote this type.

Type 4: Ratio – Common Units

•     Format: % <business-objects-1> , of <business-objects-2>

•     Implication: % <business-object><qualification> / <business-object>

•     Example: % Cost of Fulfillment, of Opex

•     Implication: % Cost of Fulfillment / Opex

•     Note: This is a ratio of two different business objects, but expressed in the same units. Because the units are the same, a % will also be used to
      denote this type.

Type 5: Ratio – General

•     Format: # <business-objects> per <business-object>
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                   585 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     Example: # Minutes per Service Problem Resolution

•     Note: This is the most general kind of ratio to express, since the numerator and denominator can be totally difference objects. The “per” is used to
      denote this type.

Type 6: Ratio – Over One

•     Description: There are some BMS ratios that are really not ratios, but needed to be expressed as a numerator over denominator of 1 for historical
      modeling reasons.

Type 7: Special Name

•     Description: Some of the metric names do not follow the above canonical rules. This occurs when a common industry name is preferred. Thus,
      rather than saying “% Customer Requests Resolved on First Call”, we prefer to use the common industry name “First Call Resolution (FCR)”.
      Since industry names exist independently of our naming convention, we denote these as Special Names.

•     Example: First Call Resolution (FCR).

Type 8: Suffix: Metric-Name-Type – By Dimension

•     Description: Generally, a dimension should not be part of a metric definition intrinsically. Rather, the dimensions should be listed out in a separate
      field, like (Prose or XLS) Dimensions. But we permit this due to historical reasons: some of the metrics already in the Scorecard are pre-
      dimensioned. But going forward, we advocate that metric dimensions should be extracted from the definition proper, and kept in a Dimension
      field.

•     Format: <metric-name-type> , by <dimension> Type

•     Example: # Bills Issued, by Delivery Type

•     Note: A dimension phrase is always started with a comma and the word “by” and ends with the word “Type”. Other uses of the word “by” may
      exist, and these are not dimensioned metrics (like “% Problem Reports Resolved by Due Date”, which does not have the comma before “by”).


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                  586 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



      Also note that a pre-dimensioned metric will have multiple values, distributed by the values of the dimension. So, the above example metric will
      have values for each of the different Delivery Types, like paper invoice, e-bill, etc.

Type 9: Suffix: Metric-Name-Type – By Dimension Value

•     Description: A few metrics are explicitly dimensioned by just one-dimension value. For example, rather than metric by “Service Type”, it is a metric
      “By Voice Services” only. The construction is similar to the preceding, except there is no “Type” at the end.

•     Format: <metric-name-type> , by <dimension-value>

•     Example: % Revenue, by Voice Services

Other Patterns

Note that other common patterns also recur within the text of the metric names:

•     If a business object’s time between two states is being measured, expressions like “from <state1> to <state2>” appear, as in “# Hours for all
      Orders, from Ordering to Activation”.

•     Generic metric words that can be used everywhere (like Average, Mean or Total) are avoided. The word Mean is actually reserved for the
      benchmarking reports – as in the mean of a metric given that the sample data has come from many CSPs. Otherwise, without these conventions,
      we would end up having a “Mean of a Means”, which is a bit unwieldy.

•     To distinguish the time for one instance of a business object versus the aggregate time for all instances, the word “All” is used, as in “# Hours for
      All Orders” (an aggregate) versus “#Hours per Order” (an average or mean).

Business Object Taxonomy

Many of the BMS metrics are about process, and as such, what we are measuring, the measurand, is process, or more broadly speaking, an
occurrence. Some measurands are people or stakeholders (# customers, # FTE), while other are artifacts, tangible or informational (# problem
reports, # data records). Of course, many are financial in nature ($ cost, $ revenue). A list of business architecture domains will help standardize


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                  587 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



these measurand categories, which will give us another way to organize, navigate, and analyze metrics. Measurands are substantives, or nouns in
our business architecture depictions. Some people call them business objects.

A taxonomy for measurands and other substantives will help limit and control the number of nouns (and ideally states and behaviors) that are used in
the metric definitions. For this iteration, our focus has been on the substantives only. We are basing the top-level business architecture domains on
the work of the OMG (Object Management Group) BASIG (Business Architecture Special Interest Group) – who have produced a domain model. The
top-level measurand domains are:

•     Capability

•     Stakeholder

•     Money

•     Artifact (a document, information record, or other business object, like a bill, a payment, a problem report, etc.)

•     Occurrence (an initiative, process, behavior or event, that takes place in time)

•     Resource (a tangible or intangible material used to deliver products/services to customers).

The list of measurands used in this release of the BMS metrics is as follows:

•     Capability

     - assurance

     - repair (a subset of assurance)

     - repair and maintenance (a subset of assurance)

     - billing

     - channel (may overlap Marketing & Sales and CRM)

     - customer management (referred to as CRM earlier)

     - fulfillment

     - sales (a subset of the Marketing & Sales capability discussed earlier).

•     SLA management (a subset of assurance)

     - Stakeholder

     - Customer

      Note: The term “customer” is preferred over “user” or “subscriber”

     - User

      Note: This term is retained for some special uses, such as its use in ARPU, where U stands for User

     - Employee (NOC FTE).

     - asset

     - billing charge

     - bill

Using this taxonomy, for example, the metric “Average Handle Time” has a measurand of “occurrence.customer_request”, since it represents the time
to handle a customer request, which is a kind of business occurrence. The list of measurands will help keep the list of nouns used in metrics to a
manageable level.

Resource Performance Management Metrics

Resource Performance Management metrics provides deep insights into the performance of the entire communication network resources and plays
important role in assessing operational efficiency, evaluating customer’s experience and conducting operational troubleshooting.

The scope of Metrics for Resource Performance management is very extensive as it includes a wide range of telecommunication domains, such as:
Mobile, Fixed, Transmission, Cables, etc. and a large variety of technologies within each one of them.

Equipment vendors may provide dozens to thousands of Metrics per a version of the equipment or per function. The list of provided Metrics per
version is usually updated in each new version of the equipment. As a result, it is common for a Resource Performance Management system to be
updated few times a year, either to keep up with frequent changes in Metrics or as a consequence of the evolving needs to monitor new equipment
added.

In modeling the world of resource performance metrics, a significant distinction to be made is between Metrics that are being measured (also known
as Measurements) and Metrics that are being calculated by one of the OSS systems. Typically, a Measurement will be taken by a Network Element
or a Probe. It will be then sent to a Management system (usually EMS, sometimes NMS) that may send it to another management system (most
typically NMS). Each one of the management systems in the OSS layered architecture may add calculated Metrics to provide additional value and
better insight of the network. Such calculated Metrics will be often termed as KPIs (Key Performance Indicators).

When identifying a Resource Performance Metric, the scope of the Metric has to be determined by its dimensions. Network typical dimensions usually
include:

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                          592 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



•     Domain/Main Technology: such as Mobile Core, Mobile RAN, Switching, Transport, etc.

•     Specific Technology: This may be a sub-category of the Domain/Main Technology. For example, for Mobile RAN domain, this may include: GSM,
      UMTS, LTE

•     Monitored Network Element/Function (Monitored Object Class level)

•     Additional technology specific dimensions

When a Resource Performance Metric is presented, a decision has to be made about the right level of the Metric as reflected in the possible values
for the composing dimensions. Currently, different standard organizations defined standard Metrics for the different Domains/Main Technologies;
therefore, it makes sense to separate the Metrics at this level. For Specific Technologies and Monitored Network Elements/Functions, these scoping
decisions should be probably further examined.

The following example may demonstrate the decisions that have to be taken when classifying a Metric. Let’s look at the “IP Throughput” Metric. We
can certainly say the following:

•     “IP Throughput” may be used for various network domains, so the option of defining it as a single multi-domain Metric may be raised. Yet, we will
      probably prefer to differentiate “IP Throughput” of a Transport equipment from the one of a Mobile E-Utran

•     A similar question can be raised about differentiating “IP Throughput” among various network elements of the same domain that use IP as their
      communication protocol.

•     For IP communication, it makes sense to differentiate between “Downlink IP Throughput” and “Uplink IP Throughput” so we may actually have
      another specific dimension in this case, based on the direction of the traffic.

•     Interestingly, “IP Throughput” can be calculated for specific “IP Links” or per groups of “IP Links” and not just for a network element object.
      Moreover, “IP Throughput” may be calculated for additional dimensions that are not even entirely network level such as: “Source
      Device/Application”, “Target Service/Application” and many more. These should be probably considered as different Metrics.

To continue this example, we may observe that we can look at average throughput, maximum Throughput, Minimum Throughput or a Peak Hour
Throughput. Each one of these will be considered as a different Metric as it has a different meaning and may be used at a different context. We can
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                  593 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



therefore see that different ways of calculating a Metric may generate a different Metrics when the result is used in a different way and has a
different meaning.

In general, there are the basic properties that are common to all kinds of Metrics such as: Metric Name, Description, Metric Unit, etc. Some properties
are specific to the way that Metrics that are Measurements are being measured. These may include a collection method and additional details on the
collection. Calculated Metrics will have a set of properties that define the way a Metric is being calculated, a formula, its components, etc.

A Resource Metric may be collected or calculated per different time granularities with a lot of diversity depending on their usage. For near real-time
monitoring or for ongoing maintenance, the time window may start with a multi-second time window up to X Minutes, hourly or daily granularity, with
most common 5 minutes or 15 minutes granularities. Metrics that are used for Resource planning may perform aggregative calculations that reach
daily, weekly or monthly granularities.

Resource Metrics can be classified by various ways. A useful way was introduced by ITU-T in E.800 document: Quality of telecommunication
services: concepts, models, objectives and dependability planning – Terms and definitions related to the quality of telecommunication services. The
document is presenting a classification of KPIS to Quality of Service categories.

ITU suggested the following QoS characteristics:

Speed

Performance criterion that describes the time interval that is used to perform the function or the rate at which the function is performed. (The function
may or may not be performed with the desired accuracy.)

Accuracy

Performance criterion that describes the degree of correctness with which the function is performed. (The function may or may not be performed with
the desired speed.)

Dependability

Performance criterion that describes the degree of certainty (or surety) with which the function is performed regardless of speed or accuracy, but
within a given observation interval.
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                594 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



Availability

Availability of an item to be in a state to perform a required function at a given instant of time or at any instant of time within a given time interval,
assuming that the external resources, if required, are provided.

Reliability

The probability that an item can perform a required function under stated conditions for a given time interval.

Simplicity

Ease and lack of complexity in the benefit to the user of a function of the service.

3GPP has referred to the ITU adding some specific categories such as “Retainability”, “Mobility”, “Integrity” and “Accessibility” categorization (3GPP
TS 21.905: “Vocabulary for 3GPP Specifications”.). Following are some examples for possible QoS classification:

•     “Success Rate/Failure Rate” of sessions at different levels of protocol are considered as “Accessibility” Metrics

•     “Drop Rates” for Voice/Data/Video are considered as “Retainability” Metrics

•     “Latency”/”Delay” of messages are “Integrity” Metrics

•     “Mobile Handover Success Rate” is a Mobility Metric (This category is Mobile specific)

•     “Availability” (percentage) is an Availability Metric

•     “Throughput” is an “Integrity” Metric.

Additional categorizations other than by Quality of Service are of course possible. A distinction should be made between categories that define the
Metric from descriptive categories that add details on the Metric.

Characterization of Existing Metrics Models-------------------------

4.19.39.1.      Figure A-1 Core concepts of OMG Structured Metrics Metamodel

Overview of OMG SMM

SMM was designed to specify metrics for software artifacts (programs, code, modules). It arose from the Architecture Driven Modernization Task
Force of OMG. They are primarily concerned with analysis and characterization of software artifacts (e.g., programs, code libraries, software
modules, etc.)

As such it is a static model, with no concept of combining measurements across time.

The assumption is that the system being analyzed is comprised by a set of elements, which are instances of a metamodel. This "target" metamodel
defines classes and associations that express the structure of the system being analyzed by a SMM model.

Figure A-1 shows the core concepts of SMM.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                         596 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Figure A-1 Core concepts of OMG Structured Metrics Metamodel




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                        597 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



4.19.39.2.      Figure A-2 SMM Measure Relationships

A Measure expresses the specification of a calculation that can be evaluated against a set of measurands to create an instance of a Measurement.
A Measure has a Scope, which specified a single class of element expressed in the Meta model for the system being analyzed. SMM defines a
"measurand" relationship between the Measurement and a MofElement, which points at a particular instance of a class in the target Meta model.

An SMMModel is comprised by a set of observations. The Observation class represents some of the contextual information which may be unique to a
measurement, such as date, measurer, and tool used. The ObservationScope class represents the model(s) or sub models that are the subject of the
related observation.

The binding of a Measure to a particular Measurement is expressed by the ObservedMeasure relationship class, which ties a Measure to a set of
Measurements resulting from an Observation, on a set of related measurands, evaluated at an instance of time.

A Measure has a Scope, which specified a single class of element expressed in the Meta model for the system being analyzed.

A Measure is associated with a single named Characteristic. The Characteristic class represents a property or trait of the members in its scope, a set
of MOF Elements, which may be characterized by applying a measure to those members. Characteristic provides for a representation of a hierarchy
of measures based upon the abstraction of measured trait. For example, a length characteristic may be the parent of the fileLength and
programLength characteristics.

A MeasureCategory has measures and other categories as its elements. A category represents the measures directly associated with an ‘element’
and the measures of each sub-category likewise associated with an ‘element.’ A measure may appear in multiple categories. A category can be a
subcategory of other categories indicating only that its measures also are measures of these other categories. This class may be used to represent a
family of similar measures that apply to different scopes. The category of a metric may indicate the kind of purpose for which the metric is used.

When a SMM model is run against a set of software artifacts, the measurands are the targets for evaluating the SMM Model to determine the values
of a set of Measurements expressed as instances of the SMM metamodel.

A Measure expresses the specification of a calculation that can be evaluated against a set of measurands to create an instance of a Measurement.

MeasureRelationship is an abstract class representing any relationship between two measures.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                             598 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



The MeasureLibrary class represents libraries of measures. A library of measures defines a reference set of measures that can be applied over and
over in a way that is independent and decoupled from the models under observation.

The structure of the Measure relationships is expressed in the SMM metamodel, as shown in Figure A-2.




                                                       Figure A-2 SMM Measure Relationships

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                         599 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



4.19.39.3.       Figure A-3 SMM Measurement Relationships

A DimensionalMeasure produces a single numerical value, expressed using a unit of measure. SMM specifies three subclasses of Dimensional
Measure:

•     A Direct Measure is one which does not involve a composition of subsidiary measures. It applies an operation to the measured entity.

•     A Binary Measure is one which evaluates a binary expression (i.e., sum, difference, ratio) involving two subsidiary Measures.

•     A Collective Measure represents measures which when applied to a given entity accumulates measurements of entities similarly related to the
      given entity. For example, it can evaluate an accumulator function across a set of subsidiary Measures taken on measurands which are
      components of a parent measurand. The accumulator functions specified are sum, maximum, minimum, average, and standard deviation.

The subclasses of MeasureRelationship express the relationships necessary to evaluate Binary and Collective Measures.

The measurandQuery association end of MeasureRelationship specifies a query that is used to determine the measurands that satisfy the relation
between two measures.

SMM also defines a parallel structure of Measurement relationships, as shown in Figure A-3.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              600 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Figure A-3 SMM Measurement Relationships

4.19.39.4.      Figure A-4 Severity Indicating Gauge threshold examples

Overview of OSI Systems Management Metric Objects

ISO/IEC 10164-11: 1994 | ITU-T Rec X.739 INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY – OPEN SYSTEMS INTERCONNECTION – SYSTEMS
MANAGEMENT: METRIC OBJECTS AND ATTRIBUTES http://www.itu.int/rec/T-REC-X.739-199311-I/en

The OSI-Management Metric Objects specification, unlike the OMG SMM, can work without an underlying metamodel, and it specifies run time
objects which perform statistical calculations on a single attribute of an observed managed object over time, using a periodically invoked get
operation. The concepts and mechanisms of this specification are applicable to any Cloud Computing environment which has capabilities for getting
values of attributes of Cloud Computing Resources. Any REST based protocol could be used by a Metric Object to scan the values of the observed
resources.

The underlying targets for the Metric objects are instance of Managed Objects in a running system. OSI-System management has the CMIP protocol
which conveys Create, Replace, Update, and Delete operations on an agent process which reflects these management operations in a real world set
of Managed Elements, represented as managed objects. CMIP has a simple get operation to get the current value of any attribute of a managed
object instance, identified by a unique distinguished name.

Metric Objects specification was designed to define metrics for statistical calculations over time, employing the concept of a scanner.

The data necessary to compute the observed values are extracted from the observed managed objects. The observed values are monitored by
sampling at an interval specified by a granularity period.

A Monitor Metric is a scanner that periodically gets the value of an observed attribute of a single Managed Object instance that it points at, with
attributes for a managed object instance pointer, the measured attribute name, and a granularity period.

The Metrics objects model assumes that observed attribute being scanned is either a counter (monotonically increasing integer value) or a gauge
(value of a varying quantity). There are mechanisms in the metric objects to derive a gauge from an attribute that is a counter (counter difference
package), as well as well as to derive a derivative of a gauge from an observed attribute that is a gauge.

There are also optional scanner mechanisms to specify different scheduling and control capabilities, for the times when scanning of the observed
attribute are active.
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                               602 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



Metric objects can also have a severity indicating gauge-threshold attribute type which is used to trigger notifications for monitoring resources. There
can be multiple threshold levels (each with an associated clear threshold) applied to the derived value in a metric object.

Figure A-4 shows an example of a severity indicating gauge model.




                                                  Figure A-4 Severity Indicating Gauge threshold examples


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              603 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




4.19.39.5.      Figure A-5 Inheritance structure of metric objects

Several subclasses of scanner, each deriving gauge values using various statistic algorithms, are specified.

The Scanner class has an attribute for the granularity period

The Monitor metric class adds attributes for the observed Managed Object pointer, the name of the observed attribute, and the derived value.

The Mean Monitor class adds attributes for the Moving Time Period (MTP) over which values are scanned, and the derived estimate of the mean
taken over the MTP. The algorithm used to specify the mean is not specified.

The Algorithm Indicating Mean Monitor class adds an attribute which specifies the algorithm used to calculate the estimate of the mean.

The Moving average mean monitor class explicitly specifies using an Exponentially Weighted moving average (EWMA) algorithm to calculate the
estimate of the means.

The three subclasses of Moving Average Mean Monitor class add EWMA estimates for percentile, variance, and min/max statistics.




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                           604 of     931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Figure A-5 Inheritance structure of metric objects

4.19.39.6.      Figure A-6 NIST Metrics Model

Overview of the NIST Cloud Computing Metrics

NIST RATAX-CloudServiceMetricsDescription-DRAFT-V2.3d9 http://collaborate.nist.gov/twiki-cloud-
computing/pub/CloudComputing/RATax_CloudMetrics_WAs_Docs/RATAX-CloudServiceMetricsDescription-DRAFT-v2-3d9.pdf

The NIST Metrics model defines terms and concepts which can be represented by Templates which can be used to formally define metrics for cloud
computing.

The Model does not assume the availability of an underlying Structured model for the system being measured. As such the binding of the Metrics to
real world entities and entity types is informal.

The following terms are defined for the NIST Cloud Computing Metrics model.

Measure (noun)

Value that expresses a qualitative or quantitative assessment of a property of an entity. Is produced as result of a Measurement and is conforming to
a Measure Definition.

Note: The definition above is based on the definition of Measure in ISO/IEC 15939:2007. As a result of concerns regarding alignment with other
Metric models, which use the term Measure for the abstract definition of the process of measuring, the NIST Metrics WG is considering changing this
term to "Measurement Result".

Measure Definition

A definition of the Measure(s) produced in assessing a property of an entity

Measurement

Set of operations having the object of determining a Measure

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                            606 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



Note: Based on the definition of Measurement in ISO/IEC 15939:2007. As a result of concerns regarding alignment with other Metric models, the
NIST Metrics WG is considering removing this class from the Model, since the semantics are captured by the combination of the Metric and the
Measurement Definition concepts.

Metric

A standard of Measurement for a particular Measure Definition, which defines the conditions and the rules for performing the Measurement.

A metric can be understood as a standard set of procedures and rules for generating values for its associated measure definition.

Stakeholders define the scenario of use for a metric. The scenario represents the expectations of an underlying business or operational process (e.g.
SLA or Operation) and how the metric will be used to assist such process, what range of values represent acceptable performances of the measured
cloud service properties. The scenario also defines the way the metric will be applied – what Cloud resource or service is being measured, under
which conditions is the metric evaluation triggered, frequency of the evaluation, etc.

The metrics define the cloud service properties to be measured, their related measure definitions, parameters, formulas and constraints
(measurement rules, context conditions).

Typically, a metric is based on a primary measure definition, but may involve other secondary – or underlying – measure definitions, that are
subordinate to the primary – i.e. are used by the definition of the primary measure. The measures define the measurement results of the properties
observed.

The UML class diagram for the NIST metrics model is shown in Figure A-6




© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                            607 of    931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0




                                             Figure A-6 NIST Metrics Model
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                         608 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



4.19.39.7.      Figure A-7 Refinement of NIST Cloud Metrics model reflecting on-going work

In the definitions below the term “element” refers to a UML Class of the model and the term “attribute” refers to a UML Attribute of a UML Class.

ContextCondition

A ContextCondition element is expressing the conditions under which this metric is applicable, in a real environment or context.

The description attribute is a summary of a particular condition of applicability.

The targetCharacterization attribute characterizes the type of Cloud resource(s) to which this metric applies, i.e. the type of resource of which some
property can be measured. For example, an availability metric might be exclusively defined for “storage services”, and therefore would not be
appropriate to measure the availability of a compute server or of a network.

The applicabilityRestriction attribute is expressing particular constraints to be observed when using the metric. For example, measurement rules in
the metric definition may depend on some regulatory environment (e.g. a particular definition of “business time”), which in turn might reduce the scope
of applicability to certain countries.

Measure

A Measure element holds the necessary information to understand a value resulting from a measurement of the attribute being observed.

The value attribute represents the result of the observation.

The unit attribute represents the unit of the value at the time of measurement.

The uncertainty attribute represents the uncertainty at the time of measurement.

A Measure element is associated to one MeasureDefinition element. It determines the nature of the Measure element.

Both uncertainty and unit attributes carry run-time values that should be related respectively to the uncertainty budget derived from the measurement
rules and the measure definition unit metadata.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                             609 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



MeasureDefinition

A MeasureDefinition element holds the basic information necessary to understand the property to be observed.

The name attribute represents the measure definition.

The referenceId attribute identifies uniquely the measure definition.

The scale attribute gives information on how the measurement value can be interpreted and what sort of operations can be performed on it.

The type attribute represents the kind of the measure.

The unit attribute represents the base unit that will be associated to MeasureDefinition element.

The expression attribute represents the function used to assemble the underlyingMeasureDefinitions that compose the MeasureDefinition.

The underlyingMeasureDefinitions association represents the possibility that a MeasureDefinition element can be composed of other
MeasureDefinition elements. This means the MeasureDefinition element expression is using the outcome of other MeasureDefinition elements.

MeasurementRule

A MeasurementRule element is used to further constrain some parts of a Measure element and indicate possible method(s) for measurement. For
instance, an “AvailabilityDuringBusinessHour” Metric element could be defined with a scope that constrains some piece of a generic “Availability”
Measure element to limit the observation period to defined business hours. Measurement rules are necessary for the measurement

The description attribute is a summary of a particular measurement rule.

The method attribute represents the method of measurement of the targetMeasure.

The uncertainty attribute represents the estimate associated with the targetMeasure. It can be used to help determine the Metric accuracy or
precision.



© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                          610 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



A measurementRule element can influence more than one targetMeasure element (targetMeasureDefinitions association). For instance, a
measurement rule could define that all targeted time measures be of unit second.

Metric

A Metric element helps define a cloud service property to be measured, its related measures, parameters, formulas and measurement rules so it can
be used in a given context.

The name attribute represents the metric.

The referenceId attribute represents a unique way to identify the metric in a repository.

The category attribute represents the type of the metric (i.e. business, technical, security).

The parameterList attribute lists a set of variable names that can appear at various places under the Metric element – e.g. in some measurement
rule or measure definition. The parameterList attribute can be what is needed by an implementer of the Metric element – to use the variables or
attribute names inside the list either as parts of a run-time Metric interface, or in a Metric configuration file.

A Metric element is associated to one or more ContextCondition elements (contextConditions association).

A Metric element is associated to one or more MeasurementRule elements (measurementRules association).

A Metric element is associated to one primary MeasureDefinition element (primaryMeasureDefinition association).

Based on feedback from reviewers regarding alignment with other models for Metrics, the NIST Metrics WG is considering changing the name of the
Measure class to MeasurementResult, and is removing the Measurement class from the model. The MeasureDefinition class along with the Metrics
class adequately captures the semantics of the specification of the calculations used to derive a measurement result. Figure A.7 is a class diagram
reflecting current work in the NIST Metrics WG.



The Model in Figure A.7 is a refinement of the current working draft of the NIST Metrics definition specification. This refined metrics model removes
the Measurement class and changes the name of the Measure class in Figure A-6 to MeasurementResult. It represents the relationship that a Metric
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                            611 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



can have with underlying metrics, and also has classes representing of Measure parameters, Measure parameter instances and measure rule
instances.




                                       Figure A-7 Refinement of NIST Cloud Metrics model reflecting on-going work
© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                     612 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



4.19.39.8.      Figure A-8 Example of Use of Refined NIST metrics model to represent response time metrics

Figure A-8 is an example of object instances following the refined model in Figure A-7, representing an example of the actual specification of some
response time metrics.

The ultimate goal of this work is to specify a set of templates which can be used to define actual Cloud Computing metrics.

Comparison of the Metric Models

This section describes how each of the Models summarized in the preceding sections represent concepts regarding Metrics and their evaluation.
They present a high-level view of the differences between these models.

Representing kinds of target for a metric definition

In OMG SMM the Scope class has a pointer to a single named class in the MOF metamodel for the target system.

NIST Metrics Model has an attribute targetCharacterization in ContextCondition class. However, the NIST Metrics model does not assume a model
for the target systems is available.

In TM Forum SID Metrics, the relationships between MetricDefinition and RootEntityType and Specification convey the target for a Metric. SMM
Scope is not restricted to a single class from the underlying system model.

OSI Systems Management Metric Objects are installed at run time, and there is no restriction to what classes of Managed object can be observed by
a Metric Object.

Binding a measurement to a specific target for a single metric evaluation

In OMG SMM the Measurement class has a pointer to a single MofElement instance in the target system.

The NIST model currently only specifies definitions applicable to use in Metric templates. There are no pointer attributes in the Measure class (to be
renamed MeasurementResult) to indicate the run time target for the Metric evaluation.


© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                             613 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



The TM Forum SID Metrics model has an association "MetricCollectedFor" from Metric class to RootEntity class. This can point at an instance of any
element defined in the model for the target system.

OSI Systems Management MonitorMetric and its subclasses have a pointer to the observed managed object instance, and its attribute being
observed.

Periodic scanning of a target by a Metric

This is the primary purpose for the OSI Systems Management Metric Objects. The MonitorMetric class has a granularity period for subsequent scans
(using a get operation) of attribute values of Observed managed objects. The MeanMonitorMetric class has an attribute to specify the moving time
period over which the estimate is calculated. The metric objects also have optional attributes to specify scheduling of when the Metric object is
enabled to scan.

OMG SMM and TM Forum SID metrics model are static in nature. The assumption is that the Metric will be evaluated for the state of the system at
the time of observation. Neither of these models specifies facilities for evaluating statistical quantities over time.

The NIST Metrics model is also a static model. The concept of periodic scanning of resource attributes is not currently supported. There is no way
specified to force a measurement to be taken using a periodic test stimulus. The examples assume measurements will be taken upon natural stimuli
(i.e., not driven by a test driver). However, the group is investigating how statistical quantities (such as mean and variance) can be evaluated from a
set of measurement results taken on a single target over some time interval (e.g., calculating a mean response time for a target over a time period
using all available response time measurements taken for that target over that interval).

Representation of a metric Measurement result

OMG SMM has the Measurement class to represent the result of a measurement.

NIST Metrics model currently has the Measure class to represent the result of a measurement. However, the NIST WG is considering changing the
name of this class to MeasurementResult, to avoid confusion.

TM Forum metrics model has the Metric class to represent the result of a measurement.



© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              614 of   931
    GB922 Common
    Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



OSI Systems Management Metric Objects have a derivedValue attribute in the MonitorMetric class which stores the current value associated with the
Metric. The subclasses also add attributes for the estimate of the statistical quantity taken over a Moving Time Period.

Representation of a metric Measure Definition

OMG SMM has the Measure class to specify the definition for a measurement.

NIST Metrics Model has the MeasureDefinition class to specify the definition for a measurement.

TM Forum metrics model has the MetricDefinition class to specify the definition for a measurement.

OSI Systems management does not allow arbitrary measurement definitions. The behavior of the Metric objects is restricted to operations over time
on observed attributes, and is hard coded into the behavior definitions for the Metric objects.

Representation of composite metric measures definitions and measurement results

The OMG SMM specifies:

•     A BinaryMeasure class which evaluates a binary operation (e.g., +, -, /, *) on the values of two other Measures. More complicated
      combination operations need to be expressed by breaking the calculation into a series of binary operation steps.

•     A CollectiveMeasure class which evaluates an accumulation operation (sum, min, max, mean, standard deviation) over a set of entities which are
      involved in a similar relationship with a scoped class.

•     No other forms of calculating composite metrics are specified.

In the NIST Metrics model a primary MeasureDefinition can be composed using underlying MeasureDefinitions and an expression in the primary
MeasureDefinition).

In the TM Forum Metrics model, a MetricDefinition can be composed using other MetricDefinitions through the use of
MetricDefinitionMethodArgument.

OSI Systems management Metric Objects has no facilities to compose multiple metrics.

4.20. Performance ABE
Represents a measure of the manner in which a Product and/or Service and/or Resource is functioning.

Performance ABE Introduction

This paragraph describes the SID Service and Resource Performance ABEs. This performance model could be extended to also support the SID
Product Performance ABE. These ABEs were developed to support the SID principle of inherent extensibility by employing SID modeling patterns.

The ABEs concerned by this paragraph are:

•     in Product Domain: Product Performance

•     in Service Domain: Service Performance

•     in Resource Domain: Resource Performance

•     in Common Business Entities Domain: Performance

The Service Performance ABE collects, correlates, consolidates, and validates various performance statistics and other operational characteristics of
customer and resource facing service entities. It provides a set of entities that can monitor and report on performance. Each of these entities also
conducts network performance assessment against planned goals, performs various aspects of trend analysis, including error rate and cause
analysis and Service degradation. Entities in this ABE also manage the traffic generated by a Service, as well as traffic trend analysis. This is
important for newer technologies that separate data, control and management functions for a given Service.

The Resource Performance ABE collects, correlates, consolidates, and validates various performance statistics and other operational characteristics
of Resource entities. It provides a set of entities that can monitor and report on performance. The entities in this ABE provide physical, logical, and
performance information. Each of these entities also conducts network performance assessment against planned goals, performs various aspects of
trend analysis, including error rate and cause analysis and Resource degradation. Entities in this ABE also manage traffic in a Resource. This
includes statistics defining Resource loading, and traffic trend analysis.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                              630 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



4.20.1.         Figure PF.01 - Basic Performance Entities

The Performance ABE is an addition to the SID model’s Common Business Entities domain. It represents a generalization of attributes and
relationships that are common to both the SID Service Performance and Resource Performance ABEs’ entities, such as performance indicators,
thresholds, and consequences. Resource performance entities is collected hop by hop (between two network elements), while service performance
is an aggregation of multiple resource performance instances that constitute the path a service takes through a network.

Two key entities that comprise the Performance ABE are PerformanceSpecification and Performance. They represent an application of the SID
EntitySpecification/Entity pattern. A PerformanceSpecification and its related specification entities provide the definition of the performance attributes,
such as KPIs, dimensions, and performance thresholds. The Performance entity and its related entities provide the values associated with the
manner in which a service or resource is performing or has performed, such as the loss of 5K packets between two network elements.

Following Figure depicts the PerformanceSpecification and Performance entities along with the two types, or subclasses, of performance-related
entities relevant to the model, ServicePerformanceSpec and ResourcePeformanceSpec, along with their related instance entities,
ServicePerformance and ResourcePerformance.

4.20.2.         Figure PF.02 - Service, Resource, and Performance

The following Figure shows the associations that performance entities have with existing service and resource entities, as well as the association that
the Performance entity has with the Customer entity.

Note that the dark-shaded entities, CustomerFacingServiceSpecification and CustomerFacingService are not within the scope of the model as they
related the Service domain with the Product domain, which is out of scope at this point in time. Also note that the ResouceSpecification is related
directly to the PerformanceSpecification entity and Resource is related to the Performance entity, since both Service and Resource performance deal
with Resources.

The Service Performance ABE collects, correlates, consolidates, and validates various performance statistics and other operational characteristics of
customer and resource facing service entities. It provides a set of entities that can monitor and report on performance. Each of these entities also
conducts network performance assessment against planned goals, performs various aspects of trend analysis, including error rate and cause
analysis and Service degradation. Entities in this ABE also manage the traffic generated by a Service, as well as traffic trend analysis. This is
important for newer technologies that separate data, control and management functions for a given Service.

The Resource Performance ABE collects, correlates, consolidates, and validates various performance statistics and other operational characteristics
of Resource entities. It provides a set of entities that can monitor and report on performance. The entities in this ABE provide physical, logical, and
performance information. Each of these entities also conducts network performance assessment against planned goals, performs various aspects of
trend analysis, including error rate and cause analysis and Resource degradation. Entities in this ABE also manage traffic in a Resource. This
includes statistics defining Resource loading, and traffic trend analysis.

The associations with the specification entities provide part of the definition of the performance specification entities. For example, the specification of
a GGSN and SGSN define the types of network elements whose performance is measured.

The cardinality of the association between ResourceSpecification and PerformanceSpecification is 0,2 because at most two types of resources’
performance is defined by a PerformanceSpecification. Similarly, the association between Resource and Performance also has a cardinality of 0,2.
These Resource domain entities are directly related to the respective Performance ABE entities and not to ResourcePerformanceSpec and
ResourcePerformance. The reason is that Resources are used to measure both Service-related and Resource-related performance.

© TM Forum 2023. All Rights Reserved                                                                                                 632 of   931
 GB922 Common
 Information Framework (SID) Suite R23.0.0



For the ServiceSpecification entity the cardinality is 1 because a given instance of a ServicePerformanceSpec entity measures the performance of a
single ServiceSpecification entity, such as a mobile voice call. Similarly, the cardinality of the association between a Service and a Service
performance is 1.

The cardinality between a Customer and Performance is 0,1 because an instance of Performance may or may not be a measure of performance for
an instance of Customer.
